data.title: BBC licence fee to approach £200 a year
data.description:
The cost of the BBC licence fee will surge to almost £200 after the next election as a further one million households stop paying the levy.

Official forecasts from the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) show the licence fee will rise from its current rate of £174.50 to just under £197 by the end of the decade if it continues to rise in line with inflation.

Meanwhile, the number of licence fee payers is expected to fall by more than one million to 21 million.

It comes after new figures revealed the BBC lost more than £1bn from licence fee evasion and cancellation last year.

A further 300,000 households stopped paying the levy in 2024 as audiences increasingly shun the BBC in favour of streaming rivals such as Netflix and Disney.

Overall, 3.6 million now say they do not have a licence because they do not need one, according to a report by the public accounts committee. Meanwhile, one in eight users now evade payment despite using the BBC’s services.

The figures underscore the crisis facing the BBC as it heads into negotiations over its future funding model amid a crisis over bias in its reporting.

The public service broadcaster is in disarray after The Telegraph published a dossier exposing how bosses had failed to address internal concerns about bias, including a Panorama documentary that misleadingly edited Donald Trump’s speech on the day of the Capitol riots.

The controversy led to the resignation of Tim Davie as director general and Deborah Turness as the broadcaster’s head of news. Samir Shah, the BBC chairman, who was grilled by MPs this week over his handling of the crisis, is also under scrutiny.

The scandal has raised questions over the BBC’s future as it enters into negotiations with the Government about the renewal of its Royal Charter, which expires in 2027.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she will consider all options including reform of the licence fee, though she has insisted ministers will ensure the corporation has sustainable funding.

Nevertheless, Ms Nandy is currently considering whether to block an increase in the licence fee by almost £7 to over £180 in the spring as households are already struggling with rising food and energy bills.

The Culture Secretary must decide by February whether to allow the inflation-linked rise to go ahead as planned.

Ministers have previously intervened in the licence fee. In 2022, the Conservative government froze the levy for two years, after which it was agreed that the fee would rise in line with inflation for the remainder of the Charter period.

The BBC’s licence fee income dropped by 30pc in real terms between 2010 and 2020 as a result of the squeeze on its funding.

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: Dutch back down in stand-off with China over chip factory seizure
data.description:
The Dutch government has backed down in a row for control of a crucial microchip manufacturer to China after a standoff triggered a crisis at European carmakers.

Vincent Karremans, the Dutch economic minister, said the Netherlands dropped an order seizing control of Nexperia to its Chinese owner Wingtech following negotiations with Beijing.

The Netherlands seized control of Nexperia in October, invoking a Cold War-era law, while its chief executive, Zhang Xuezheng, was ousted by a court.

A separate court decision in the Netherlands, that placed Wingtech’s shares in Nexperia under control of an independent administrator remains in effect, Nexperia said.

The Dutch government accused the company’s Chinese owners of “serious governance shortcomings” that threatened the supply of critical microchips to Europe.

According to Dutch media reports, officials also feared that Nexperia’s trade secrets had been stolen by China and it was planning to shift production out of Europe.

The move triggered a crisis in the European car industry, which is heavily reliant on Nexperia’s microchips.

China halted exports of chips to Europe while staff at the company’s Chinese division started refusing instructions from its headquarters on the continent.

Some of Europe’s biggest carmakers warned the supply chain shock could force them to shut their factories.

However, after an intervention this month by Donald Trump, the US president, China said it was prepared to ease an export ban on Nexperia’s technology.

On Tuesday, Mr Karremans said it was now the “right moment to take a constructive step by suspending my order under the Goods Availability Act regarding Nexperia”.

He said: “We are positive about the measures already taken by the Chinese authorities to ensure the supply of chips to Europe and the rest of the world. We see this as a show of goodwill. We will continue to engage in constructive dialogue with the Chinese authorities in the period ahead.”

The crisis has prompted calls for Labour to intervene at a Manchester chip plant that is also controlled by Nexperia.

Nexperia was previously forced to offload a factory it owned in Wales over national security concerns about its Chinese parent company.

Nexperia executives previously denied its ownership of the Welsh plant presented a national security risk. It did not comment on the Manchester claims.

Sir Iain Duncan Smith, a former Conservative Party leader, told The Telegraph last month: “The same rules should apply. I don’t understand why this hasn’t been resolved by the Government.”

A Wingtech spokesman said the Dutch intervention in Nexperia had threatened the “continuity of Nexperia and therefore the economic security of the Netherlands and Europe – which is the exact same argument the Dutch government made previously in support of judicial intervention”.

The spokesman added: “Minister Karremans justified his actions by accusing Nexperia’s CEO of various acts of alleged mismanagement.

“Wingtech strongly rejects these accusations and points out that, to date, no proof has been provided.”

data.title: How the Bank of England can secure its place at the heart of the next monetary revolution
data.description:
This morning, the Bank of England will set out its last consultation on a regime for the regulation of systemic stablecoins. How the UK pursues the regulation of digital money will determine whether London joins the US in leading the next era of financial infrastructure – or watches others set the pace. 

To its credit, the Bank’s position on the regulation of systemic stablecoins has evolved. Perhaps most remarkably, the Governor has explicitly acknowledged that the holding of money can be separated from the provision of credit – a profound shift in how we think about the structure of the current financial system. 

With the 180 day UK-US Taskforce for Markets of the Future, the UK has a rare opportunity to shape how innovation in money can preserve financial centre competitiveness.

The Governor has said that stablecoins aspiring to serve as money must be backed by “risk-free” assets. That principle is sound, but not one applied to traditional bank deposits. The Governor himself recognises that commercial bank deposits – with fractional backing – are not risk-free. 

In fact, stablecoins backed one-for-one by central bank cash reserves and other (credit) risk-free assets are less risky than uninsured deposits – and their regulatory treatment should reflect this.

The Bank’s willingness to allow a portion of reserves to be held in high quality liquid assets (HQLA) like gilts, rather than exclusively in central bank, deposits is welcome progress. A sensible approach would see a significant majority – perhaps over 80 per cent – in HQLA.

This has three advantages. First, it creates a viable pathway for issuers to transition between the FCA (non-systemic) and Bank of England (systemic) stablecoin regimes, without the need to fundamentally change business models. Second, it introduces new demand for short-dated gilts, supporting the UK Government’s funding base and lowering borrowing costs. Third, it aligns the UK regime with that of other major jurisdictions, making the country a more attractive destination for stablecoin innovation.

Concerns have been raised that in a crisis a run on a stablecoin issuer could trigger a fire-sale of gilts. If that’s the case, the Governor’s suggestion that the Bank could offer Emergency Liquidity Assistance (ELA) is one way to address this. 

The Bank also looks set to propose caps on stablecoin holdings, supposedly to guard against a “precipitous” drain of deposits from banks into digital money. 

Other jurisdictions offer a useful test case. In the United States, the dollar denominated stablecoin USDC has operated for nearly six years without evidence of a destabilising flight from retail deposits. In fact, in the 70 months of data from USDC, we observe that both retail deposits and USDC’s market cap have increased during that time. 

Empirical evidence suggests neither caps, nor a ban on stablecoins paying interest are justified. By proposing both, which no other jurisdiction has deemed necessary, the UK risks putting the brakes on innovation. 

The focus should be on facilitating modernisation, not privileging legacy systems. The Bank’s proposals sit awkwardly with both the Government’s competitiveness strategy and firms’ obligations under the Consumer Duty. 

Furthermore, it is unclear how caps on holdings would be implemented once a stablecoin transitions from the FCA’s regime for non-systemic stablecoins to the Bank of England’s regime once it becomes systemic - would there be forced liquidations? Second, the caps proposed are so low that it would make stablecoins unusable for wholesale settlement in tokenised markets. 

The rationale for caps is therefore weak, and industry needs clarity on when – and on what basis – those caps would be lifted. The Governor has said it is important to “think through the implications of change before going ahead”. But there is also risk in excessive caution. We should not want the UK to deliver a regulatory framework for stablecoins that deters innovation, at the same time as other markets move ahead.

The US has legislated to provide regulatory clarity on stablecoins, and the market has responded with around 80 different announcements of companies building in this space. 

The Bank’s evolving approach to stablecoins includes some strong and welcome progress. However, more can be done. In short, with prudence and ambition, a competitive, well-regulated stablecoin regime can strengthen financial stability and secure London’s place at the heart of the next monetary revolution. 

Faryar Shirzad is Chief Policy Officer at Coinbase

data.title: Asda boss blames botched IT upgrade for sales slump
data.description:
The boss of Asda has blamed a botched IT upgrade for a fresh slump in sales at the debt-laden supermarket.

Allan Leighton, Asda’s executive chairman, said the company had suffered “severe disruption” as a result of a £1bn plan to disentangle its computer systems from those of former owner Walmart.

The troubled overhaul – dubbed “Project Future” – led to empty shelves, online delivery issues and outages on the company’s new website and app. It prompted a 2.8pc decline in sales in the three months to the end of September.

Mr Leighton said: “The sales performance is nothing to do with competitors, it’s all to do with Future. It’s not as if everybody started to invest twice as much money, spend twice as much marketing and do all that stuff.”

He said the process had been “suboptimal to say the least”, blaming the problems on poor integration and insufficient testing. The issues have set the company back in its turnaround efforts by six months, Mr Leighton added.

The veteran executive, who was credited with leading a turnaround at Asda in the 1990s, has been attempting to revive the supermarket’s fortunes ever since his return a year ago. Earlier this year, he launched a price war aimed at enticing back shoppers.

But Asda’s problems have only deepened since then, with the grocer’s market share dropping to a record low of 11.6pc, according to the latest industry data.

While Asda remains Britain’s third largest supermarket chain, German discounter Aldi is now closing the gap. Analysts predict it could overtake Asda in as little as six months.

Asda has struggled ever since its 2021 takeover by private equity group TDR Capital and petrol station tycoons the Issa brothers, which saddled the supermarket with billions in debts.

Mr Leighton played down concerns about the declining grocery market share, saying: “We don’t just sell food, we sell merchandising, fuel, pharmacy, you name it.

“So I’m interested in how we’re doing in all of those markets individually, rather than in one market particularly.”

The Asda boss also took aim at Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, following further tax raids in this week’s Budget.

He said: “We are a country that is stuck in reverse and growth is everything that is required. To grow you’ve got to invest. The Government talks a lot about growth but what actually is it doing about it?

“At the moment business is on the back foot. All it’s doing is another load of costs it has to mitigate.”

Asda’s total revenues in the third quarter stood at £5.1bn, down almost 4pc from both the previous three months and the same period last year.

The supermarket said operational issues were beginning to subside and said availability of products was back above 95pc.

The company added that it would accelerate its investment in price cuts in the final three months of the year. It is also investing £12m to upgrade a string of major stores in Yorkshire and surrounding areas.

Mr Leighton is under pressure to demonstrate progress in his turnaround plan as rising interest rates drive up the cost of servicing the company’s £3.8bn debt pile.

The supermarket chain, which is owned by TDR Capital, inked a recent £600m deal to sell and leaseback dozens of supermarkets to help pay down debt.

However, the transaction has increased Asda’s rent bill and was condemned by union bosses as “asset-stripping”.

In a further blow, ratings agency Fitch this week downgraded Asda’s debt further into junk status, threatening the company with higher borrowing costs.

data.title: John Lewis considers opening new stores for first time in nearly a decade
data.description:
John Lewis is considering opening new department stores for the first time in nearly a decade.

Peter Ruis, the managing director of John Lewis, said expansion was “definitely something we are looking at” and suggested the company was plotting moves into new parts of the country.

He told The Guardian: “The store is a perfect invention, and we’ve seen only too well, coming back from Covid, how people have gravitated back to the stores.”

Any new launch would be the chain’s first since its shop in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, opened in 2018. A spokesman said there were no immediate plans for new store openings.

It comes as John Lewis seeks to reinvigorate the business after closing 16 sites and cutting thousands of jobs following a pandemic-induced slump in sales.

The 161-year-old employee-owned department store chain lost money for three years in a row before returning to profit in 2023.

Mr Ruis, who previously served as John Lewis’s fashion boss, returned to the company last year to lead turnaround efforts.

The retailer has set out plans to spend £800m refurbishing its 36 remaining stores by 2029. The flagship John Lewis store on Oxford Street reopened last year following a major overhaul.

Bosses had previously planned to convert almost half the floor space into offices. Still, they opted to retain all six floors after a stronger-than-expected rebound in shopping following the pandemic.

Other locations that have benefited from a facelift include those in High Wycombe, Buckinghamshire, and Cheadle, Greater Manchester. Meanwhile, the store in Bluewater Shopping Centre in Kent recently reopened after a £10m makeover that included a new “gifting emporium”.

Mr Ruis said he was aiming to ensure that John Lewis’s physical stores remained relevant to shoppers. He said: “We are getting rid of the old stuffy department store and replacing it with something more experiential.”

Innovations include opening Waterstones bookshops in-store and introducing new brands such as Topshop and Charlotte Tilbury.

John Lewis also plans to expand its partnership with Uber Eats, allowing customers to order beauty products and gifts directly to their door.

It comes ahead of a crucial trading period for John Lewis, which is relying on an increase in Christmas sales to balance the books after its losses fell to £88m in the first half of the year.

The retailer has previously blamed Labour’s tax increases, including higher National Insurance contributions and a new packaging levy, for the losses.

However, it has targeted better results in the second half of the year.

Mr Ruis said: “I think we’re optimistic, but, you know, it’s a difficult market. It’s difficult for the customer.”

data.title: A shortage of seafarers threatens Trump’s fossil fuel boom
data.description:
Donald Trump’s attempt to double US exports of liquefied natural gas (LNG) within five years faces an unexpected threat: the waning popularity of life at sea.

The US president plans to make LNG production the cornerstone of a new fossil fuel boom, symbolised by his promise to “drill, baby, drill”. To get production pumping, Trump wants to flood the world with American LNG – a fuel Western countries have increasingly come to rely on in the wake of Russia’s war in Ukraine.

His plan is spurring demand for hundreds of new ships over the next decade, needed to transport the liquefied fuel. Yet a lack of seafarers to man the vast new fleet may render that ambition an impossibility.

The shipping industry is in the grip of a recruitment crisis as nations that have traditionally supplied crews to merchant vessels see their young populations increasingly turn away from the ocean for more comfortable employment ashore.

One senior oil executive says recruitment of LNG crews may be one of the biggest obstacles facing Trump’s planned energy boom.

They are not the only one who is concerned. “It’s not attractive for people in developed countries – and in particular Gen Z – to spend such a long time away from family and friends, whatever the pay,” says Martin Roolvink, the managing director at Pronav, which provides more than 500 crew for LNG carriers.

Roolvink says the majority of officers on Pronav’s books are Croatian or Ukrainian – but even in those places, becoming a seafarer “is not that popular any more”.

Work-life balance is an issue since LNG terminals, which store and convert the fuel, are usually located far from population centres. Ships’ crews, meanwhile, have little opportunity to visit towns and “probably will not see anything shoreside for three months”, Roolvink says.

Compounding the problem is the fact that LNG – comprised mostly of frozen methane – is among the most dangerous of cargoes, representing both a fire risk and a threat to the vessels that carry it, given the risk of cold-induced hull fractures.

The huge storage tanks on LNG vessels also give them a high “windage area”, which makes them harder to handle in stormy conditions and high seas, according to Shyam Paliwal, founder of Tank Terminal Training.

Sailors seeking to move into the sector from traditional cargo vessels – such as container ships, bulk carriers and even oil tankers – must gain extensive qualifications and undergo years of on-the-job training.

Roolvink says: “LNG carriers are much more complex than bulk or container vessels. They have specific technical features – including very sophisticated systems for automation, cargo containment, tank systems and propulsion – which require highly trained personnel.

“Loading and discharging are critical operations. Once you are connected to the shore terminal, there is a huge amount of attention required with 24/7 watches.”

Firms have resorted to poaching seasoned seafarers from liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) carriers, the nearest thing to LNG vessels but without the ultra-high pressure, -160 degrees Celsius storage.

Throwing money at the problem, though, has pushed labour costs towards unsustainable levels. Experienced LNG ship masters command as much as $20,000 (£15,000) a month to captain vessels on short-term contracts.

The hiring squeeze is likely to persist as more vessels hit the water. The number of ships designed to transport LNG currently stands at around 750 but is forecast to surpass the 900-strong global oil tanker fleet for the first time next year.

Shipping broker Clarksons reckons 451 new LNG carriers worth a total of $103bn will be ordered this decade – though producing the ships will itself be a challenge with barely half a dozen shipyards capable of constructing them, mostly in South Korea.

Global exports are forecast to reach record levels next year and it’s expected that trade volumes will surge 60pc by 2030, making LNG a mainstay of global energy consumption.

Exports will be led by the US and Qatar, which are expected to control 50pc of LNG supply by the end of the decade, with European and Asian nations the biggest importers.

Evidence of the boom can be seen in the rapidly rising cost of transporting LNG. Carriage rates hit $170,000 per day on the London market this month, the highest for two years (rates reached an eye-watering $400,000 a day in the immediate wake of the Ukraine invasion).

The surge in demand could translate into a requirement for as many as 18,000 LNG-qualified seafarers, according to some forecasts.

Roolvink reckons the sector will need to find at least 3,500 senior officers alone if it is to keep pace with anticipated rates of growth. He says: “It’s one of the biggest concerns of the sector and it means planning is essential.

“The companies involved must ensure that the people are trained and that they have a long-term perspective, otherwise they will not be able to man the ships.”

LNG has boomed in popularity partly because of its versatility. While LNG ships are much more high-spec than other vessels, the fuel is still easier to transport than natural gas, which can only be moved over long distances via pipelines.

It has also been enthusiastically embraced by governments as a “transition fuel”: it produces less carbon dioxide than coal or oil and provides a bridge to future reliance on renewables.

Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022 spurred a surge in demand, with LNG credited with keeping the lights on in Europe as Moscow slashed traditional gas supplies.

Now Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” approach to fossil fuels is set to take supply to new highs.

The president signed an executive order bearing the title Unleashing American Energy on his inauguration day, overturning a ban on new applications for LNG exports imposed by Joe Biden in 2024 amid pressure from climate activists.

The US only began selling LNG overseas in 2016 but had become the world’s largest exporter by 2023, prior to the Biden moratorium.

Trump’s intervention has paved the way for up to 20 new LNG terminals – the facilities for storing and converting the fuel – in the US, in addition to the eight that are fully operational and three already under construction.

Whether there are enough people to staff them remains to be seen.

data.title: This is the Chancellor’s biggest lie yet
data.description:
Of all the fake news that tumbled out of the Chancellor’s mouth on Budget day, the notion that this Government is about to restore some sense of normality to Britain’s broken energy system is perhaps the biggest lie of all.

Not that your typical family would know any better of course, which is precisely the point. To the vast majority of households, £150 off an annual energy bill is not to be sniffed at, especially during these straitened times.

The move helps to paint a picture of a Labour Government looking out for working people, not to mention honouring a key manifesto pledge to make energy more affordable.

Factor in a further £150 towards your energy costs through the Warm Home Discount for those on benefits and bills might start to look even more manageable for the poorest in the country.

Indeed, add the two together and it is the view of some Labour MPs that the Government will soon be able to claim that it has met its promise to cut energy bills by £300 – at least for the six million or so people that fall into this camp.

It truly is desperate stuff from a party heading for oblivion. The reality is that Labour deserves no applause whatsoever for this cynical attempt to pull the wool over our eyes.

The proposals laid out by Rachel Reeves at the despatch box amount to financial sleight of hand of the very worst – a cynical confidence trick played on unsuspecting voters without the knowledge to spot what is happening.

Yes, there’s a £150 reduction coming, but to pretend that this is some sort of permanent adjustment that means we’re heading to a world where it will be cheaper to heat our homes and turn on the lights is totally disingenuous.

What the Chancellor announced on Wednesday is nothing more than a short-term reprieve from an energy system that has become a dangerous mess and where bills are only going to get more prohibitively expensive.

What’s more, it is a situation that has been largely created by catastrophic policy decisions from a Government that has no idea what it’s doing. So for Labour to be asking everyone to be thankful for “bringing down energy bills” and easing the cost of living – as Reeves claimed straight-faced on Wednesday – really does take some beating.

The Treasury’s £150 saving is nothing more than a ruse designed to make people look the other way, while Labour piles on yet more levies and crushes domestic production with its decimation of the North Sea, sending bills further into the stratosphere in the process.

The basis for the £150 reduction is fairly straightforward once you lay it out. The Government is ditching the so-called energy company obligation, a scheme that forces energy suppliers to fund energy efficiency measures such as home insulation for low-income households. That will save a typical household £67 a year, experts say.

Plus, it was introduced under the Cameron Government, so Labour presumably thinks it gets bonus points for laying some of the current unaffordability at the door of the Opposition.

Then there’s a further £78 annual saving through the partial removal of the renewables obligation – effectively another green levy to pay for the construction of wind farms.

Yet the part that the Chancellor has shamefully chosen to be less forthcoming about is the long list of green levy increases barrelling down the track – increases that are bound to wipe out any savings over the long-term.

Still, at least the Office for Budget Responsibility – fresh from its attempt to torpedo the Budget entirely – is living up to its name and keeping count.

It notes that the cost of the capacity market – which pays for gas fired power stations, battery parks and hydro-electric plants to be on stand-by in the event that wind and solar plants fail – will jump from £1bn to about £4.5bn over the next five years.

As the OBR wonks point out: “Renewable energy sources ... have more variable energy generation compared to non-renewable sources.”

Similarly the costs of Contracts for Difference – yet more subsidies which fund wind and solar plants – will more than double from about £2bn this year to north of £4bn by 2030.

Then there’s the cost of financing Sizewell C in Suffolk, expected to spiral from £300m today to £1.4bn at the end of the decade, plus the Warm Home Discount that will add a further £1.1bn to annual bills.

Add in the estimated £150 rise in bills due to the cost of upgrading the national grid and the picture is much clearer: energy costs are set to go through the roof as a direct result of this Government’s insistence on going hell for leather with renewables.

Again, this is laid out in black and white by the OBR. It calculates that the cost of green taxes on energy bills will surge from £14bn this year to £19bn by 2030 under Reeves’s Budget proposals. Twenty years ago, receipts from environmental levies totalled just £300m.

As the OBR neatly puts it, “this reflects a shift away from the taxation of energy extraction to taxation on the consumption of energy”, so voters should be in no doubt about what is happening here.

It wouldn’t be quite so bad if Labour possessed the foresight to smooth the so-called energy transition with home-grown oil and gas reserves – but instead, the Government has embarked on a wilful destruction of the North Sea at the same time.

Faced with a series of anti-oil and gas policies, including the windfall tax, offshore operators are abandoning the region in their droves in what is set to trigger a near-collapse in tax receipts. Tax revenues from North Sea oil and gas will plummet ninefold from £2.7bn to just £300m between 2025 and 2030, the OBR says.

It also means the UK will become increasingly reliant on more costly imports of fossil fuels from the likes of Norway, Qatar and the US at a time when it already boasts the most expensive electricity prices in the world. The UK grid operator has warned that the amount of gas produced by the North Sea will decline by over 80pc this year alone.

The effect of all this then is to keep bills higher than they need to be at the same time as massively undermining energy security. How any of this makes sense is one of the great puzzles of our time. In the hands of Sir Keir Starmer, Reeves and Ed Miliband, this country is firmly on the path to self-destruction.

data.title: Chinese EV giant to build one of Europe’s biggest car factories
data.description:
A Chinese electric vehicle (EV) maker is scouting for locations to build one of Europe’s biggest car factories.

Great Wall Motor is looking at sites in Spain and Hungary along with other countries as it targets a production output of 300,000 vehicles per year locally.

Assuming most of the carmaker’s production is concentrated there, the factory would be one of the largest in Europe.

Britain’s largest plant, run by Nissan in Sunderland, has a maximum capacity of 600,000 but in practice has been producing far fewer vehicles than this recently.

Volkswagen’s Wolfsburg plant in Germany is the biggest in Europe, with a maximum theoretical capacity of around 900,000 per year.

Amid brutal competition on pricing at home, Chinese carmakers have been piling into the European market where they have been able to undercut more established brands.

For example, BYD has increased its sales from about 36,000 to 138,000 in the first 10 months of the year while SAIC Motor, the owner of MG, has gone from about 198,000 to 250,000. The European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association (ACEA) does not publish figures for Great Wall Motor.

Parker Shi, the president of Great Wall Motor International, said his company had a target to export one million cars globally by 2030. The target includes 300,000 cars in Europe.

Mr Shi told Reuters, which reported the announcement: “That’s why we’re speeding up the European strategy. Everything needs to speed up.”

In the UK, Great Wall Motor is selling the electric ORA 03, a five-door hatchback, for upwards of £21,000, along with the Haval Jolion Pro compact SUV for about £22,000 and the POER300 pickup truck for about £31,500.

By comparison, electric versions of the Mini Cooper and the Vauxhall Corsa – which are similar in terms of performance – start at around £28,000 and £27,000 respectively.

But as Chinese firms’ sales have grown, so has anxiety in European governments about the threat to local jobs and regional brands such as VW and Renault.

The companies have stolen a march in particular on EVs, which they are able to produce at significantly lower cost than their European counterparts.

There are also concerns about the massive amounts of state aid lavished by Beijing and Chinese local authorities on their own car manufacturers through cheap loans, vehicle mandates, charging infrastructure and grants.

Against that backdrop, the European Union last year imposed tariffs on imported Chinese EVs.

That has spurred the companies to look at local production lines. However, to avoid tariffs, they will have to meet certain “rules of origin” requirements that demand more than simply shipping parts to Europe and putting them together.

Mr Shi said Great Wall Motor was keeping a close eye on EU policies, including emission rules designed to spur the sales of EVs.

“It’s going to be a huge investment for a long term,” he added.

data.title: Gulf royalty strikes deal to build thousands of London homes
data.description:
A Dubai-based property developer owned by Middle Eastern royalty has struck a deal to build thousands of homes alongside the River Thames.

Arada, co-founded by Sheikh Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi of Sharjah, a city in the United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia’s Prince Khaled bin Alwaleed bin Talal, has acquired an 80pc stake in Thameside West, a 47-acre development project in east London.

The scheme is set to deliver at least 5,000 homes near Royal Victoria Dock, and is estimated to be worth £2.5bn once completed.

It signals the growing influx of Middle Eastern money into London, particularly in the property sector.

Arada acquired the stake in Thameside West from London developer Keystone, paying an upfront fee of £225m alongside a pledge to invest a further £100m.

Keystone will own the remaining 20pc of the site, which is nearly twice the size of Hudson Yards in Manhattan, New York.

The acquisition is also one of the biggest land deals in London since 2020, giving the capital a much-needed boost after a recent slump in demand.

Just 5,933 new-build private homes were sold during the first three quarters of 2025, according to findings published by consultancy Molior London last month, with sales “weak across all local markets and at every price point”.

Dwindling sales have also led to a drop in construction, with activity hampered by high costs and uncertainty prompted by this month’s Budget.

Recent estimates show that just 15,000 to 20,000 private homes are to be under construction in London by the end of 2026.

‘Dubai model’

However, Ahmed Alkhoshaibi, the chief executive and co-founder of Arada, is confident that demand will recover.

“We will build and it will come,” he said. “That’s been the Dubai model. We will build even if there isn’t demand upfront.”

Mr Alkhoshaibi added that it is “the right time” to invest. He said: “It’s always the best time to buy when the price of the land is down as a developer.

“To create spaces that are on another level, you need to buy at the right price. Right now, London is down, but we’re confident it will be up sooner than later.”

It comes after Arada bought London housing developer Regal for £230m in September, with the aim of building 30,000 homes in the capital over the next three years.

During the 1990s, Prince Khaled was known for enjoying a lavish lifestyle, reportedly owning 200 luxury cars.

However, the guilt he experienced from taking part in a trophy hunt in South Africa in the latter part of the decade prompted him to adopt a more pared-back lifestyle – selling off his collection, becoming vegan and driving just one car, a Tesla.

His family also owns nearly 50pc of the Four Seasons international hotel brand, with the other half owned by Bill Gates.

data.title: Britain threatened by gas shortages as North Sea output plummets
data.description:
Britain faces the risk of severe gas shortages as plummeting North Sea production leaves the country dangerously reliant on imports, the UK grid operator has warned.

In a report published on Wednesday, the National Energy System Operator (Neso) said gas production was poised for a steep decline because of Labour’s energy policies – posing a risk to Britain’s energy security as the grid becomes in hock to foreign suppliers.

Neso’s analysis came after it assessed the impact of Labour’s ban on new exploration and the 78pc tax rates imposed on North Sea gas producers.

According to the energy body, gas availability will be 78pc lower by 2035 compared with today – equivalent to a decline of up to 13pc a year.

It also warned that the amount of gas produced by the North Sea this year will decline by over 80pc – from 27 billion cubic metres (bcm) to just 5bcm by 2035 – making the UK massively reliant on supplies from Norway, the US and Qatar.

“During periods of peak demand in the 2030s, we expect GB’s import dependency could rise above 90pc,” said the report.

“Our analysis identifies an emerging risk to UK gas supply security. In scenarios where all gas supply and network infrastructure are operational, this risk is evident only where peak gas demand remains close to or above current expectations.

“However, in the unlikely event of the loss of the single largest piece of gas infrastructure, gas supply falls short of demand.”

The reference to gas infrastructure refers to the potential failure of the pipelines feeding Norwegian gas to the UK or to a liquefied natural gas (LNG) terminal going offline.

Norway supplies nearly half the UK’s gas via North Sea pipelines – but it too is running out, with production set to halve between now and 2035, Neso warned.

The findings contradict Ed Miliband’s pledge to make the UK less reliant on imports and on “petrostates”.

In September, the Energy Secretary told the Labour Party conference: “We’re at the mercy of the petrostates and dictators. And the only way out of it is our mission for clean power by 2030.”

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “This report should be a wake-up call. It shows that the UK is in the midst of a gas supply crisis made in Whitehall by Ed Miliband and Rachel Reeves, who have created an unprecedented, policy-driven decline of North Sea gas production.”

Chris Wheaton, an analyst at Stifel, said Neso could even be downplaying the threat: “We think Neso forecasts are too optimistic. We forecast gas production to decline by 70pc by 2030 due to the impact of the windfall tax.

“That suggests the UK will run out of LNG import capacity as early as 2031, as the current import terminals reach the maximum rate they can deliver gas into the UK grid during winter. The best way to ensure security of gas supply is to maximise domestic gas production.”

The Neso report points out that gas demand surges in winter and can hit peaks of 482 million cubic metres of gas per day (mcm/day). Even by 2035 when decarbonisation will have been long under way, it predicts winter demand hitting 422mcm/day.

But by then, North Sea contribution will have fallen to about a fifth of what will be needed just for the UK’s gas-fired power stations. In Britain, 27 million homes rely on gas boilers for heat and hot water.

Ashley Kelty, from Panmure Liberum, said: “Current government policy makes no sense. Its energy policy is driven by misguided and ignorant net zero dogma which places more importance on virtue signalling than energy security.

“We saw the dangers of over-reliance on imports when Russia invaded Ukraine – this should be a lesson to move to rely on domestic supplies rather than imports.”

Richard Tice, Reform UK’s energy spokesman, said Britain’s plight was caused by poor policy rather than geology: “We need to increase UK oil and gas exploration by reducing the energy profits levy and motivating investment with smarter regulation removing lots of unnecessary rules.”

data.title: Hinkley Point C nuclear power station will add £1bn a year to energy bills
data.description:
The troubled Hinkley Point C nuclear power station will add £1bn annually to UK energy bills as soon as it’s switched on, official figures show.

The money will be taken from consumers and handed to the French owner EDF to subsidise operations, making it one of the UK’s most expensive sources of electricity.

A further £1bn will be added to bills by a separate nuclear levy, supporting construction of the Sizewell C nuclear power station in Suffolk, also led by EDF. Campaigners branded it a “nuclear tax on households”.

Details were revealed in documents released by the Treasury and the Office for Budget Responsibility in the wake of Rachel Reeves’s Budget.

They describe how EDF will be entitled to claim the money under the “Contracts for Difference” subsidy system as soon as Hinkley C begins operations, probably in 2030.

The documents state: “In 2030-31, Contracts for Difference (CfDs) are expected to generate £4.6bn in government receipts, including £1bn to fund subsidy payments to the Hinkley Point C nuclear power plant for its first year of expected generation.”

The impact on bills is linked to a 2013 agreement reached between EDF and Sir Ed Davey, the then energy secretary. He guaranteed that EDF could charge £92.50 per megawatt hour (MWh) of power once Hinkley Point C came online. With inflation, this equates to £133 today and is expected to reach about £150 in 2030.

If the wholesale cost of electricity remains at its current level of about £80/MWh, then EDF can claim an extra £70 from consumers and businesses via CfDs.

From January, energy bills will also be hit by an entirely separate levy designed to support the construction of another nuclear power station at Sizewell in Suffolk.

The Regulated Asset Base levy will add £10 a year to power bills from 2026, raising £700m, but will roughly double by 2030, when it will need to raise £1.4bn a year for Sizewell.

Official modelling suggests Sizewell C’s final costs could reach £100bn.

Bills could rise even further once approval is given for another nuclear site on Anglesey in north Wales, where the UK’s first small modular reactors are planned.

‘We remain opposed to the imposition of a nuclear tax’

Alison Downes, of Stop Sizewell C, said nuclear power had proven to be among the most expensive ways of generating electricity.

She said: “The Government has a misguided belief that nuclear will be a cheap, ‘green’ solution to our energy needs, but the evidence shows the opposite – that costs of delivery and of dealing with nuclear waste – will continue to rise.

“We remain opposed to the imposition of a nuclear tax on households, given the acknowledged uncertainty about the projected costs of constructing Sizewell C.”

The surging cost of CfDs is part of a much broader increase in energy-related environmental levies. These add £14bn to energy bills at the moment, but this will rise to £19bn by the end of the decade, according to Treasury forecasts.

The CfD subsidy system, which also supports wind and solar, currently costs consumers and businesses £2.3bn a year, but those costs will hit £4.6bn by 2030-31.

Meanwhile, the Renewables Obligation, which funded the UK’s first wind farms, adds costs of £8bn a year.

The Capacity Market, which pays gas-fired power stations and hydroelectric facilities to be on standby in case wind and solar are hit by low wind and light levels, adds a further £1.6bn.

A government spokesman said the subsidies were necessary to unleash its planned “golden age of nuclear power”.

They said: “Hinkley Point C and Sizewell C will each deliver clean electricity for six million households for at least six decades. Sizewell C could save £2bn a year across the future low-carbon electricity system once operational, resulting in cheaper power for consumers.”

Hinkley C was initially forecast to cost £18bn, but spiralling construction costs have pushed the total bill to £46bn, equivalent to £700 per person in the UK.

EDF was contacted for comment.

data.title: Mike Ashley denied vote on Boohoo boss’s £150m bonus
data.description:
Boohoo has blocked Mike Ashley from voting on a £150m bonus scheme for its chief executive.

The development comes as the online retailer launches an attempt to turn around its languishing share price.

The board of Boohoo, which was recently renamed Debenhams Group, said it intended to approve the scheme without a shareholder vote, which is a typical process for listed companies.

It will mean Mike Ashley, who is Boohoo’s largest shareholder with a stake of almost 30pc through his retail empire Frasers Group, will be barred from voting on the proposals.

In an update to investors on Thursday, Boohoo said that a “major competitor” and a “significant shareholder” had set out to “disrupt Debenhams Group’s growth strategy and operations” by voting against a series of measures at past shareholder meetings.

Mr Ashley has been locked in a bitter row with Boohoo’s board and its founder, Mahmud Kamani. The Sports Direct tycoon has accused Mr Kamani of ignoring the best interests of investors and, in August, attempted to oust him and two other members of Boohoo’s leadership team.

He also attempted to block Boohoo’s decision to rename its business as Debenhams earlier this year. Mr Kamani and Mr Ashley first clashed in 2021 over control of the collapsed department store chain.

Boohoo said that while it had previously sought shareholder support for its management bonus schemes, it believed that “approval at a general meeting would not be most likely to promote the success of the company”.

Bonus scheme turnaround efforts

Under the proposed bonus plan, Daniel Finley, Boohoo’s chief executive, would receive a maximum possible payout of £148.1m if the company’s share price rises from 10.5p to £3 over the next five years, and is maintained at £3 over the final three years.

The suggested payout is part of a wider bonus scheme that will see senior leaders share up to £222m if they can rebuild the company’s valuation from £145m to £4.2bn.

Boohoo, which also owns Karen Millen and PrettyLittleThing, was once valued at more than £5bn during its pandemic peak in 2020, but its shares have since collapsed as it struggles to compete with rivals such as China’s Shein.

Boohoo said its new bonus scheme was needed to ensure its executives could “execute the turnaround strategy over the coming years and consequently restore profitability and unlock value for all shareholders”.

It came as Boohoo posted a 23pc drop in sales to £296.9m during the first six months of the year compared with a year earlier.

However, the company also disclosed a sharp drop in losses, costs and overall debt, boosting shares by as much as 40pc on Thursday morning to around 16p.

Mr Finley said: “Our turnaround is gathering real pace. We are making progress, we are moving fast, and we are transforming the business.”

data.title: Domino’s boss departs after warning of ‘peak pizza’
data.description:
The chief executive of Domino’s has stepped down unexpectedly after warning that Britain was hitting peak pizza.

Andrew Rennie, who ran the chain for two years, will leave the business immediately after unveiling ambitious plans to tilt Domino’s towards fried chicken amid a slump in pizza demand.

Nicola Frampton, the chain’s current chief operating officer, will take over as caretaker chief executive while Ian Bull, the chairman, and senior director Natalia Barsegiyan search for a permanent replacement.

Mr Rennie’s exit comes just days after he warned that the UK’s pizza market was approaching saturation, with a supply glut of chains pushing diners towards burgers and fried chicken.

In response to the slowdown, Mr Rennie announced plans to start selling fried chicken from 1,400 British Domino’s stores through a new brand known as “Chick ‘n’ Dip” as well as looking for a new fast food chain to acquire.

On Tuesday, the pizza chain added that it will hold back from making any new acquisitions until a permanent chief executive is in place.

Domino’s, which opened its first UK store in Luton in 1985, has suffered a sharp slowdown in sales over the past two years which has dented its profits.

Its problems have been made worse by higher energy costs and the financial hit from Rachel Reeves’s increase of employers’ costs. Other pizza chains, such as Pizza Hut, have also struggled with the economic headwinds.

It has seen the FTSE 250 company’s share price slump by 51pc over the past year, as the stock has become one of the most shorted on the London stock market.

In September, hedge fund Browning West publicly urged Mr Bull to halt Mr Rennie’s acquisition plans.

The hedge fund, which owns a 5pc stake in Domino’s, instead called on the company to launch a £100m share buyback to return more money to investors. Domino’s said it now plans to “review its capital allocation priorities”.

Mr Rennie started out as a Domino’s franchisee in Australia when he was 26 years old before he was recruited by the company’s head office.

Ms Frampton previously worked at Deloitte and William Hill before joining Domino’s in 2021.

data.title: Chip giant caught in Dutch-Chinese row warns carmakers of factory shutdowns
data.description:
A major chip company that was seized by the Dutch government has warned of “imminent production stoppages” at carmakers amid a standoff with its rogue Chinese branch.

Nexperia, which produces semiconductors used across the car industry, said in an open letter to staff at its Chinese division that factories risked shutdown without a reopening of relations.

The Dutch business said it had been “compelled to communicate publicly” after its “repeated and multiple attempts, both formal and informal, to re-establish the dialogue” were ignored.

The chipmaker has been at the centre of a geopolitical crisis after Dutch officials invoked a Cold War-era law to allow it to take effective control of the business. The Government justified the move by warning that the company’s Chinese owners, Wingtech, posed a threat to Europe’s chip supplies and accused them of governance failings.

The decision prompted Beijing to impose export controls on Nexperia’s chips, while staff at its Chinese business stopped following instructions from its European headquarters.

The blockade threatened to devastate Europe’s car industry and prompted The Hague to back down. The Dutch government revoked its order earlier this month after negotiations to diffuse the crisis.

Nexperia’s technology is used in dozens of electronic components that feature in modern cars, ranging from electronic window controls to windscreen wipers.

Wingtech’s shares in the business remain under the control of a court-appointed administrator in the Netherlands and its Chinese chief executive has also been suspended.

On Thursday, Nexperia claimed its Chinese branch had continued to ignore attempts to restart normal business despite the detente between Dutch and Chinese governments.

“Nexperia has made repeated and multiple attempts, both formal and informal, to re-establish the dialogue with Nexperia’s entities in China by means of direct outreach via calls, emails and proposed meetings, various offers to collaborate on mutually beneficial solutions and even formal correspondence to demand performance of rights,” the company said.

“Regrettably, Nexperia did not receive any meaningful response. We urge you to respond to Nexperia’s outreaches and engage constructively and with a view to restoring the supply chain, as this is in the interest of all parties involved, including notably our customers.

“In light of this, we are compelled to communicate publicly to underscore the urgency.”

A Wingtech spokesman said Nexperia’s letter was “highly misleading” and said it had “repeatedly expressed our willingness to engage in constructive communication”.

The spokesman said: “We have also, on numerous occasions through multiple channels, reached out to Nexperia to pursue a negotiated solution.

“If the control dispute remains unresolved, numerous customers could face supply chain disruptions.”

data.title: Inflation calculator: how the latest rate will affect your savings
data.description:
This calculator shows the current rate of Consumer Prices Index (CPI) inflation will affect the return on your savings.

Inflation calculator

How to use this calculator

Type in the amount you have in a savings account or cash Isa, and the interest rate you’re currently receiving.

Press the “Calculate” button to see the result.

How it works

This calculator works by figuring out how much interest your savings will earn over the course of a year – this assumes interest is paid annually, and the interest rate and amount of savings remains the same.

This is pitted against the most recent CPI measure of inflation, as reported by the Office for National Statistics.

Assuming inflation remains steady, it then shows the value your savings will lose in real terms due to inflationary price increases.

The calculator will also show you what the cumulative impact of these losses will be for each of the next five years.

What is this calculator useful for?

The calculator can help you gauge whether or not it’s worth switching to a savings account or cash Isa offering a higher rate of interest.

This is particularly important when inflation is high, but in general it’s a good idea to make sure you’re taking advantage of the best savings rates to reduce the effect of price increases. If you don’t, put simply, your money won’t buy as much as it could the previous year.

You can also use the calculator to see how much of a difference it would make to switch savings accounts for a better rate.

For example, someone with £20,000 in an account paying today’s average easy-access rate, according to Moneyfacts, of 2.51pc would make a “real terms” loss of £210.42 after a year because of inflation. They would earn £502 in interest, but their pot would effectively be worth just £19,789.58 after a year when adjusted for inflation.

Switching to the top easy-access savings rate which, according to today’s tables showing the best easy access savings accounts, is from Cahoot and pays 5pc, they’d make an inflationary increase of £270.27, based on actual interest of £1,000.
