data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain risks Guinness Zero shortage over Christmas
data.description:
Britain risks a shortage of Guinness Zero this Christmas after workers at the brewer’s Belfast site voted to hold eight days of strikes.

Members of the Unite union will walk out from Friday Dec 5 until the early hours of Saturday Dec 13 in a dispute over pay.

Roughly 90 employees are demanding a substantial pay increase, complaining they are paid less than workers at parent company Diageo’s packaging facility in Runcorn, Cheshire.

The Belfast factory is a major canning facility for Guinness Zero, which is the UK’s best-selling non-alcoholic beer.

Michael Keenan, Unite’s regional officer, said: “Diageo’s Belfast site is the world’s biggest producer of Guinness Zero and this strike action will severely disrupt production lines in the run-up to Christmas.

“Management needs to stop disrespecting our members and return to the negotiating table with an offer that meets workers’ pay expectations.”

Diageo insisted there would be no shortage and said there were “contingency plans in place to manage any potential impact”.

A spokesman said: “While we are disappointed by the outcome of the ballot for industrial action at our can packaging site in Belfast, we strongly believe that continued engagement is the best way of securing a resolution that recognises employees for their valued contribution, while ensuring the long-term competitiveness of the site.”

Nevertheless, the threat of shortages will alarm those hoping for a non-alcoholic Christmas.

Guinness Zero launched in 2022 and has since become a hit with abstemious drinkers. In addition to output in Belfast, it also now accounts for about 14pc of total production at the company’s St James’s Gate brewery in Dublin.

The success of the zero-alcohol version has helped drive a wider boom for the black stuff – which has enjoyed a recent resurgence as a fashionable choice, especially among younger drinkers.

Demand was so great that Diageo was forced to limit supplies of Guinness to pubs in the run-up to Christmas last year to ensure it did not run out over the festive period.

More than 10 million pints of Guinness are sold every day, accounting for one in every nine pints poured across the UK.

In a divisive move, Guinness recently rolled out a “60/40” pint that combines Guinness Zero with the original stout to create a lower-percentage drink.

Guinness has proved a bright spot during an otherwise challenging time for Diageo, which also owns drink brands including Smirnoff, Johnnie Walker and Captain Morgan.

Shares in the London-listed company have tumbled by almost a third this year, with the company blaming the wider economic malaise for a slowdown in sales.

Diageo recently appointed Sir Dave Lewis, the former Tesco boss, as its new chief executive after predecessor Debra Crew stepped down over the summer.

Sir Dave earned the moniker “Drastic Dave” thanks to his reputation for aggressive cost-cutting and was credited with leading a turnaround of Tesco.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain’s Victorian railway embankments are collapsing. And it could cost us billions
data.description:
When a London-bound express train derailed near the village of Shap in Cumbria earlier this month after ploughing into a landslide, ministers insisted that such incidents were vanishingly rare.

What they failed to mention was that landslips on the railway have reached an all-time high, as a stormier and rainier climate threatens to damage hundreds of miles of embankments dating back to Victorian times.

With Britain’s trains busier than ever, fears are now growing that these centuries-old railway embankments cutting through the countryside could pose a serious risk to passengers – and cost billions to make safer.

Britain’s creaking railways have many problems – from overcrowding and late timetables to poor Wi-Fi and constant signal failures. Yet the dangers posed by landslides and poorly maintained embankments can have fatal consequences.

In 2020, three people died in Stonehaven, Scotland when the lead carriages of a high-speed train en route to Aberdeen plunged down a bank after hitting a landslide triggered by torrential rain.

The accident was the first to kill a passenger since 2007 and the first fatal crash caused by a train hitting a landslip since 1995.

Although last month’s Cumbria derailment was the first major landslide incident since Stonehaven, there are concerns that Britain’s railway system is poorly equipped to detect landslides and prevent further derailments.

Alarmingly, only a minority of landslides are detected by monitors set up to provide a warning when they occur.

Just 8pc of landslips – 13 of 147 – were detected via remote sensors last year, despite £33m having been spent on monitors, a freedom of information request to the Office of Rail and Road found.

In the case of the Cumbria derailment, the Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) has said that one of its chief concerns was the “effectiveness of on-site monitoring equipment”.

The spike in incidents and low detection rates has begun to raise concerns about whether remote monitoring can protect passengers – and the costs of upgrading the system if not.

Currently, the vast majority of incidents are reported by train drivers, maintenance crews and engineers.

And drivers are becoming increasingly concerned about the growing risks from landslides, some railway chiefs say.

Tricia Williams, managing director of Northern, Britain’s second-biggest train operator, said after Cumbria she was forced to reassure her drivers and train crew that it was safe for services to operate across the north of England.

Ms Williams said Northern had suffered its own near miss at Grange-over-Sands in Cumbria when the track collapsed ahead of a train as an embankment was washed away.

“We have to recognise it’s a risk, but we’ve got to keep the railway moving. The key is the monitoring equipment,” she said.

“I think there was a monitor at Shap, but there needs to be a certain number of monitors triggered. The message to my team is that they are the eyes and ears as well, and that if they see things that they are worried about, report them.”

Expensive alternative

The alternative to driver monitoring is the saturation of the entire 10,000-mile rail network with remote sensors.

But coupled with measures to shore up thousands of miles of slopes, that could cost billions.

Gareth Evans, head of Rail Technology at Network Rail, said such an approach would be so expensive as to be impractical.

He said: “We have to take a risk-based approach to understanding the earthwork assets because there’s so many of them it would not be affordable for the taxpayer to deploy instruments on every single metre.”

The Aslef train drivers’ union said that, whatever the bill, everything possible must be done to enhance safety and avoid a repeat of Stonehaven.

Mick Whelan, Aslef general secretary, said: “Stonehaven casts a long shadow and we must ensure that such an accident never happens in this country again.

“While we understand that there are many cuttings, embankments, bridges, and viaducts on Britain’s railway network, it is vital that Network Rail has the money to ensure that landslips, and other failures, do not happen.”

In the recent Cumbria incident, an Avanti Pendolino tilting train ran into a mudslide immediately south of a summit near Shap, which is the highest point on the West Coast Main Line in England.

While the train was operating at only 83mph, two-thirds of its top speed, and the landslide was shallow – estimates suggest a depth of just a few feet – the impact was sufficient to lift the wheels of the leading carriage off the rails and into the gap between the tracks.

It then ran for more than half a kilometre before coming to a standstill. Extensive damage was caused to the train and track, though just four of the 95 people on board were hurt.

At the Pendolino’s top speed of 125mph, and with a full load of 600-plus passengers, however, the outcome could have been very different.

The RAIB’s preliminary report said a drainage ditch has been flooded because of the heavy rain, leading the slope of the embankment to become too muddy and causing the landslide.

Yet, worryingly, monitors were in operation in the area but seemingly failed to detect the landslip. A Network Rail spokesman declined to comment further on concerns regarding monitoring equipment.

Sensors failure

Network Rail, which is due to be merged with nationalised train operators to create Great British Railways (GBR), is responsible for more than 190,000 individual “earthworks” – which include embankments and soil and rock cuttings.

Rock cuttings are the most likely to suffer some kind of landslide, but they can be covered up to catch any falling stones.

It is soil cuttings that have become the main focus of monitoring, which over 20 years has progressed from visual inspections through instruments drilled into the ground to lower-cost sensor technologies that directly alert rail control rooms to any ground movement.

Simon Abbott, Network Rail head of geotechnics, said that in cases where “sensors are taken out by a large failure” there are many examples of them “having worked really well”.

However, he said that monitoring is “not a silver bullet”.

“Sending people out is a resource we don’t really have at our disposal any more, so technology is playing more of a part,” he said.

Mr Abbott added: “You could put more investment into any aspect of that overall approach and see value, but it’s how you use an appropriate level of funding across the spectrum.

“We’ve got a huge amount of work that we’ve identified where we can justify intervening, and we’ve also got sites where we want to strengthen how we manage risk.”

Network Rail has also placed greater focus on weather forecasting in the wake of Stonehaven, with trains drastically slowed down in areas experiencing heavy rain.

Following the recent Cumbria incident, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, pledged that safety would remain the number one priority for the industry through nationalisation.

She said: “It is right that Network Rail are making sure they shape the way in which they are spending money to address the changing climate.

“I do want proper monitoring to be in place to make sure that we reduce disruption to a minimum and that we can keep our railways operating as safely as possible.

“I don’t ever want to be in a position again where I’m doing a broadcast round and find out that there has been a train derailment.”

However, Richard Holden, the Conservative transport spokesman, said he is concerned that Labour’s creation of GBR will erect barriers to the work needed to combat landslides.

He said: “We want to see more and better monitoring, but you get that through innovation. And to get the Treasury to invest in innovation you’ve got to have a growing rail sector.

“You need a railway which is operating busily so that the risk of not doing something can be calculated in financial terms. If stopping a popular service running is going to involve a huge amount of cost, it’s going to get national attention, as the Shap incident did.

“But as the railway is brought under state control, who is going to be fighting for resources at a time when the Government is being squeezed on multiple different fronts?”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Starmer pledges to spend billions more on drugs in NHS overhaul
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to boost NHS spending on drugs to prevent an exodus of investment from the UK.

The Government is set to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. The move will allow the NHS to pay billions of pounds more for medicines.

Several drug companies have scrapped investments in the UK amid a row over how much the Government was spending on new drugs compared to other countries.

The pricing shake-up will appease drugmakers – but it also threatens to put Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, on a collision course with Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, over how to fund the multibillion-pound bill.

Lord Vallance, the science minister – who is negotiating a new deal with the industry on behalf of the Government – admitted last month that price increases were necessary to stop a wave of investment leaving the UK.

The Times reported that the Treasury is insisting that spending increases must be funded through existing departmental budgets, while NHS bosses are demanding extra financial support to pay for any deal.

The looming showdown comes as the Chancellor prepares to unveil another tax-raising Budget, with economists predicting extra levies of around £30bn to balance the books.

Ms Reeves boosted health spending at the expense of other government departments in her June spending review, with the department accounting for 90pc of the extra money for public services over the next three years.

It means the NHS and other health spending is now on course to account for half of all spending on public services by the end of the decade.

Meanwhile, a string of companies including Merck, the US cancer drug manufacturer, and Eli Lily, maker of obesity jab Mounjaro, have halted investment in the UK over the past year in protest against the NHS’s drug pricing regime.

This week, AstraZeneca’s UK chairman signalled that the company was ready to scrap investment in Britain unless the Government backed down on pricing.

Shaun Grady warned that the UK was in danger of becoming unattractive for companies unless it brings its “appalling” drugs regime into the 21st century.

Pharmaceutical companies are also under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has escalated tensions by threatening 100pc tariffs on the sector if it does not invest in America.

The antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS has not been changed since 1999.

The system uses a formula which seeks to determine value for money based on a drug’s effectiveness.

Currently, Nice will approve medicines that cost less than £20,000-£30,000 for every extra “quality-adjusted life year”.

A new range of between £25,000-£35,000 per year is said to be one option under consideration.

However, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry has warned that the threshold would be closer to £56,000 if it had climbed in line with the cost of living. It has called for a range closer to £50,000.

A government spokesman said: “The pharmaceutical sector and the innovative medicines it produces are critical to our NHS, our economy and the plan for change.

“Through our life sciences sector plan, we’ve committed to working with industry to accelerate growth in spending on innovative medicines compared to the previous decade.

“We’ve secured a landmark economic partnership with the US that includes working together on pharmaceutical exports from the UK whilst improving conditions for pharmaceutical companies here.

“We’re now in advanced discussions with the US administration to secure the best outcome for the UK, reflecting our strong relationship and the opportunities from close partnership with our pharmaceutical industry.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The ‘bonkers’ plan to build a brand new city for one million people
data.description:
Skyscrapers made of wood, “6G” networks, solar energy and small modular reactors serving a metropolis filled with Britain’s most ambitious families.

Welcome to Forest City 1, the working title of the country’s first new city in 50 years.

Shiv Malik, a former investigative journalist, and Joe Reeve, a co-founder of Looking for Growth (LFG), a grassroots political movement focusing on expanding the economy, have hatched a £100bn plan to build a city the size of Bristol.

The project would be built on 45,000 acres of land on the Cambridgeshire-Suffolk border between Newmarket and Haverhill.

The duo hope the new metropolis will one day be home to one million people – with 12,000 acres set aside for woodland.

To some observers, the project is nothing more than a mad pipe dream that is unlikely to see the light of day. But to supporters, it is a DIY attempt solve the country’s housing crisis amid growing disillusionment with the Government, and a way to kickstart the economy.

“Let’s fight for a grander vision ... One that is independent of the central state but also not bound to the private developer mindset which itself lacks any real vision,” a website promoting the project reads.

“We need something that points the way on the world stage and lets Britain once again be a by-word for ambition, invention, creativity and entrepreneurialism.”

The proposals have captured the imagination of architects, planners and other industry figures – who have joined a dedicated WhatsApp group to discuss the project.

An online petition pledging support has gained 700 signatories, including Grant Fitzner, the director of macroeconomic statistics at the Office for National Statistics; Dame Patricia Hewitt, a former trade and industry secretary; and Professor Tim Leunig, a former advisor to two Conservative chancellors and three housing secretaries.

Suggestions put forward by supporters include: a transport system consisting of 5,000 self-driving Tesla Robovans (rather than trains or trams), roads designed to support driverless Waymo cars and covered streets resembling the porticoes of Bologna to shield visitors from Britain’s dreary weather.

‘No time to waste’

Malik admits the sheer scale of the ambition will raise eyebrows. But he says: “It comes with an element of dreaming, but people are ready to do something serious about this because the situation is so desperate.”

While the Government is seeking to address the same issues through dozens of new towns in England, Malik believes the “only way that makes sense is to build at city scale, not at town scale”.

“I think lots of people have been disappointed that we have been presented with 12 sites for new towns, after a year of real consideration by experts, [of which] this Government has only plumped for three. We don’t have time to waste any more.”

Malik says Forest City will “easily cost over £100bn”, with an estimated £200m required for the first stage, including the master planning and site surveys. The city would be privately funded.

A number of private individuals and venture capital firms have reached out to pledge funding to the project, Malik says. He and Dame Patricia met with Surinder Arora, the billionaire hotelier, on Friday.

Leunig, who advised Sajid Javid and Rishi Sunak, calls Forest City “a bold and ambitious idea” that is “exactly what the country needs”.

But not everyone is enthusiastic.

“This just is not credible,” says Jackie Sadek, a former specialist adviser to the Government on urban regeneration. “Who are these guys? Do they have any expertise at all?

“I appreciate that strange things do happen but not in the property development industry. I wouldn’t be down at Paddy Power putting money on this one.”

One senior regeneration specialist says: “I admire their energy and ambition but fear the idea is completely bonkers.

“There’s no public transport, no roads or rails. If you look at the history of where towns and cities get built, they get built where they need to be and where you can get to, so the most fundamental element is already broken.”

Owners of the land earmarked for development include Sheikh Mohammed Bin Rashid al-Maktoum, the prime minister of Dubai. It is unclear whether the Sheikh is supportive of the project.

Maxwell Marlow, of the Adam Smith Institute, a think tank, is also sceptical. “It’s wonderful thinking, I’m fully behind it,” he says. “However, do I think this is going to happen? No, because there are viability concerns about planning and policy.”

Attempt to ‘change the narrative’

Rico Wojtulewicz, of the National Federation of Builders, sees the project as an attempt “to change the narrative”.

“Governments historically don’t like to build too many homes in case house prices drop,” he says. “The one way a government can get around that is by building a city, because you set the prices and it doesn’t necessarily compete with surrounding places – it’s a place of its own.”

Frustrations with housing affordability in the South of England is a key driving force behind interest in Forest City, with property prices outstripping wage growth over the past two decades.

It has made the project’s plans for 400,000 affordable homes a compelling proposition for twenty and thirty-somethings who feel property ownership has become a distant hope.

For those happy to be a guinea pig, developers tout the possibility of purchasing a family home for £350,000. One idea is to target young people who might prefer to invest in an initiative that will provide a home once the city is built, rather than save for a mortgage.

Adrian, a 31-year-old planner from Dorset, is one of those who has signed the petition and is a member of the Forest City WhatsApp group. He is interested in becoming one of its first residents.

“I’d love to one day get married and move in somewhere, but I haven’t even started that mortgage journey, or purchasing a house or land,” he says. “The prospect gets me quite nervous – how come this has felt so closed off to me?”

The notion of a new city trying out innovative concepts is also appealing for Adrian, who believes Britain’s cultural conservatism is stifling its creativity.

“I think of England as a place which doesn’t tend to build new, unless it’s a one-off building within a city or town centre, or a set of the same Noddy ‘toy town’ houses in suburbs that have no real cohesion in terms of a sense of identity,” he says.

“Seeing people in various fields of architecture and industry, discussing ideas for a new city ... It gets me thinking, ‘Wow, I could be contributing to a new vision in England, that I could perhaps move into one day.’”

Malik says: “This isn’t about ‘building a city’. This is about solving very specific problems in the UK – housing, infrastructure, growth – which we think are best resolved via building a city.”

Parliamentary approval will be sought for a development corporation, called the Albion City Development Corporation (ACDC). These organisations are created by the Government to oversee large-scale projects and have compulsory purchase and planning powers.

Malik says a “silent majority” of people in their mid-40s and younger are “desperate for viable solutions to their very material problems”.

“It’s not all about Palestine and trans rights,” says Malik. “It’s about paying the rent and building a family.

“They just haven’t had political representation. They certainly haven’t had ambitious ideas put to them. Once you do that, it’s amazing how much support there is.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘It has a dystopian feeling’: The residents still waiting for the town they were promised
data.description:
After swapping London for a new life in Sherford, Jack Dowson noticed something peculiar. “There was nothing,” he says. “When I moved here, there wasn’t even a convenience store.”

Dowson was drawn to the south Devon town by the developers’ artistic impressions, which painted a picture of a vibrant community and a buzzy town centre.

But several months later, he says the reality of life has set in. “It has a very dystopian feeling. Nobody really gets out and does things together,” he says. “You need a car to be able to get anywhere.”

The initial plans for Sherford envisioned a walkable market town comprising 5,500 homes, shops, restaurants, schools and leisure facilities that would “become a model for town planning in the 21st century”.

But a decade on, its 3,500 residents are served by just one temporary cafe and an adjoining container-like storage unit offering essentials such as tea and biscuits, which is kept locked up unless someone wants to buy something.

Sherford is far from the only new development to adopt a houses first, amenities later approach.

Fifty minutes up the road, Cranbrook, also in Devon, and Northstowe in Cambridgeshire, initially came under scrutiny as homes sprang up while the delivery of shops and restaurants lagged well behind. Both have recently added amenities, and developers insist they are “committed to creating thriving communities”.

As Labour races to roll out its new town plans and meet its ambitious target of building 1.5 million homes by 2029, Sherford may serve as a warning.

‘A betrayal’

Poundbury, a town on the outskirts of Dorchester, was the inspiration for Sherford. Endorsed by then-Prince Charles, Poundbury bucked post-war town-planning trends by prioritising pedestrians over cars, integrating businesses with private and social housing and adhering to traditional regional architectural styles.

But after a consortium of commercial developers, which bought the project in 2014, successfully won approval to water down the prescriptive town code, critics claim the civic needs of the local population fell down the list of priorities.

Ben Bolgar, of The Prince’s Foundation (now called The King’s Foundation), the master planner for Sherford, branded the move a betrayal, adding that it had put “the wishes of the volume housebuilders ahead of the wishes of the local people”.

Today, Sherford’s nearest supermarket is a 15-minute drive along a busy main road, while most have no choice but to make the five-mile trip to Plymouth for the most basic services. The temporary community hub, also housed in a container-like structure, serves as a base for all types of meetups, from religious congregations to Girl Scout groups.

“There’s no GP surgery,” says Cat Burchell, a resident. “It’s a pain.”

As frustration grows at the sclerotic delivery of amenities, the project’s developers – Taylor Wimpey and Vistry Group – have sought to assuage concerns. In August, they said they had finally applied for a town centre, including a 4,500 sq ft convenience store, with a completion date some time in 2027.

But for those who have waited years for the bustling community they assumed would be here by now, fresh promises only highlight what they still don’t have. “It’s not just about shops. They mis-sold us on everything – an entire lifestyle that was a lie,” says Eleanor Faith, who arrived in 2022. “I truly feel I have been conned.”

‘Zombie town’

South Hams District Council commissioned The Prince’s Foundation to create a concept for Sherford in 2004. But in 2017, under pressure to meet government-mandated housing targets, South Hams granted developers in charge of the project permission to relax design restrictions.

This was despite objections from Plymouth City Council, which warned that diluting the original plans risked creating a “zombie town”.

South Hams councillor David Hancock says “the application was considered on its merits” and “allowed more flexibility to enable the scheme to adapt and evolve,” rather than reduce the level of amenities.

In the name of accelerating development, the changes included a more flexible approach to house design, more visible car parking and alterations to how streets were laid out.

Walking around Sherford, it is clear to see the physical impact of these changes to the code.

In the area encompassing the first phase of development, wide pavements are lined with handsome Georgian-style townhouses, and there are sports and children’s play facilities. On the other, less developed side, houses still under construction have a more uniform feel, with no facilities in sight.

Steve Fowler, Sherford’s project director, says that, given a project of Sherford’s timescale, the original plan would inevitably have to “adapt and evolve” over time, and says the consortium’s intention is never to mislead anyone.

The Sherford Consortium adds that it “has always been keen to show the vision for what Sherford will become – as well as being honest and transparent about what homebuyers can expect”.

It says it is “still delivering the same community facilities, new homes, and open space. The civic needs of local people remain central to delivery, as evidenced by the facilities already delivered and those in the pipeline.”

Yet with none of the key tenets required to form a strong community – or even the structures in place to house them – locals have been left feeling isolated.

“With a lack of bars and coffee shops, it just seems like everyone is apart from each other,” says Dowson. “I think people are sort of socially isolated. Everything is segmented. There’s the occasional get-together, but there is no real socialisation of the people here.”

A chicken-and-egg problem

Not everyone feels so aggrieved. Joanne Hilton, who runs Sherford’s temporary cafe, says she’s seen a growing sense of community since moving to the town in 2018. “I absolutely love it here. I wouldn’t be anywhere else,” she says. “If anything is going on, people seem to come together and help each other out.

“With any new build, all those things come later. I didn’t move here for shops, but I know all of that will come.”

Fowler blames the slow delivery of amenities on the project’s “complexity”. Difficulties in gaining commitments for sites when the town is still under construction have also been a stumbling block. For example, a temporary space will be provided for a GP surgery. Still, it will ultimately be the NHS’s responsibility to fund the rest and find doctors willing to work there.

Similarly, many retailers are often hesitant to set up shop in areas until certain occupation thresholds are met. This can create a chicken-and-egg situation, where people may be reluctant to move to an area because of a lack of amenities, but the amenities will only appear when there are enough people.

As Sherford moves ever closer to its target of 12,000 residents, arguments around thresholds are inevitably losing weight. Some of those who have lived in the town for years can no longer fathom why delivery has been so slow. For young families, the lack of a promised secondary school and youth club is proving particularly painful.

For lack of anything to do, anti-social behaviour among young people has reportedly become a problem.

As a result, some residents say their kids have been forced to avoid the few facilities that do exist.

‘You can’t just keep building houses’

Adding to the frustrations of Sherford’s shop-less residents is news of success stories elsewhere.

Nansledan, in Cornwall, has nearly 900 homes and more than 40 local businesses 12 years after work on the project began. The new suburb, which received the Duchy of Cornwall’s endorsement, was designed to relieve pressure on nearby Newquay and subscribed to the same principles as Poundbury, including walkable neighbourhoods and mixed-use buildings.

Meon Vale, near Stratford-upon-Avon, which comprises around 1,000 new homes, also has restaurants, a shop, a community centre and a business park that appeared simultaneously. “The town hall, shop and leisure centre were some of the first things to be built,” says Faith, who used to live in Meon Vale. “That is the way it used to be.”

Rebecca Smith, Conservative MP for South West Devon, says: “We face a significant challenge with the delivery of amenities in new towns and new housing developments in general. It is not unique to Sherford.”

For those who have laid roots in the town, however, all they can do now is wait. “I do like living here,” Burchell says. “But you can just see they’re building, building, building. You can’t just keep building houses. You need infrastructure in this town.”

data.title: Classic British TV is a hit around the world. But for how long?
data.description:
Midsomer Murders is known as a mainstay of British daytime TV, entertaining viewers with increasingly outlandish murders wiping out the population of a fictional English county.

Yet despite its narrow English references, the hit show also happens to be one of the UK’s biggest cultural exports, being broadcast in more than 200 countries around the world from Belgium to Bolivia.

The classic show, which launched in 1997, is no freak hit either. British television productions regularly punch above their weight on the international stage and demand is now growing for their lengthy back catalogues.

After bruising years of decline, exports of British TV programmes are back in growth and have topped £2bn for the first time, according to figures from Pact, which represents independent production companies.

Yet behind the headline numbers is a growing reliance on back catalogue – or “library” – sales, which accounted for 44pc of TV export revenues last year: a record high.

In other words, the growth of the British TV industry is driven largely by selling old episodes of programmes such as Mr Bean, Midsomer Murders, QI and Grand Designs, rather than new hits.

The reason for the popularity of classic shows is a slowdown in production of newer hits.

After a mini-renaissance of television making over the past decade, driven by the rise of streaming channels, rising production costs have prompted broadcasters to cut back their budgets.

In the UK, the BBC, ITV and Channel 4 have all reduced the amount they spend on programmes.

Similar trends are happening abroad. Between 2016 and 2024, commercial broadcasters in the five biggest European markets reduced their content spend by 19pc on average, according to research by Ampere Analysis.

Meanwhile, streaming services are tightening the purse strings after years of profligate spending at the height of the streaming wars.

Every major streaming service, including Netflix, Disney and Amazon Prime, reduced their commissioning from UK production companies last year, according to the Pact research.

As a result, cheaper back catalogue shows are becoming an increasingly popular way to fill TV schedules and streaming platforms.

“We’re going through a structural change across most of the broadcasting markets globally,” says John McVay, the chief executive of Pact.

“Normally distribution would have been driven by us licencing first-run programmes, but as people have got less money and the costs of production have gone up through inflation, what people are doing is buying more catalogue, which is cheaper.”

That’s good news for the UK because of its popularity around the world. British programmes regularly top the list of the most watched shows on Netflix, with original UK productions far outstripping any other nation in terms of viewing minutes.

The reason for this success – barring the obvious advantage of the English language – is harder to pin down.

Supporters point to the UK’s public service broadcasters, which have created a thriving TV production ecosystem. Others say there is something intangible about British culture that attracts interest from abroad.

“All things lead to some sort of innate quality or relevance relative to other production markets,” says Tom Harrington, at Enders Analysis.

“You would assume that the near universality of the English language drives it – and it can’t not be helpful, but it’s more than that.”

It’s also helpful that the big-spending US market is the largest destination for TV exports, with revenues rising by more than a third since last year.

While things may be good for now, there are concerns that these benefits could be short-lived.

Demand for UK shows in the US has, in part, been driven by the need to fill a gap left by a sharp slowdown in production in Hollywood because of Covid, strikes and the Los Angeles fires.

What’s more, the US media sector is braced for a sweeping wave of mergers that would reduce the number of commissioners in the market.

Paramount, Comcast and Netflix are all locked in a bidding war for Warner Bros Discovery, the behemoth behind Hollywood film studios and networks such as HBO and CNN.

“Everyone is already making less content and consolidation will only add to this downward trend in production volumes – nobody merges to spend more,” says Harrington.

With budgets shrinking and as the industry braces for a wave of consolidation on both sides of the Atlantic, UK producers are having to adapt, knowing they can’t rely on old hits forever.

In addition to selling more library shows, production companies are increasingly teaming up to spread the cost – and risk – of making programmes.

More radically, producers are increasing their focus on making money from non-programme sources such as merchandise, board games and books. This type of revenue rose to £212m last year, accounting for 11pc of total exports.

It is a well-established trend.

The BBC’s commercial arm makes significant money from selling Bluey toys around the world. Disney made more than £3bn in revenues last year from merchandise for Lilo & Stitch alone.

In 2023, Mattel turned its best-selling toy into a cinematic phenomenon with the Barbie movie.

Harrington is sceptical about how much money UK producers can make through this strategy.

“The one rule of TV is that no one knows if something will be a success, let alone if they will then buy a T-shirt,” he says.

And while brands have long sponsored TV series, Britain’s stricter broadcasting rules mean TV shows can’t simply be one long advert in the style of the Barbie film.

Nevertheless, as budgets tighten, UK producers know they can’t dine out on back catalogues forever and if they want to make money, they’ll have to start getting creative.

“You’re an independent IP [intellectual property] creator, you’re no longer just a TV producer,” says McVay. “The future is, you need to sweat your IP harder than you’ve ever sweated it before.”

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: To restore Britain, start with the rail dining car
data.description:
Among stiff competition, few experiences more totally obliterate any lingering faith in Britain today than the average train journey. Penned like cattle into stiff plastic seats, stifled by delays and cancellations, bombarded by youths playing music out loud and preyed upon by conductors yearning to triple charge you for what you thought was the right ticket, the average railway journey is an express train into misery.

Yet amid this litany of woe, there is the worst offender. I’ve had some depressing meals in my time, from school dinner slop to peculiar post-club kebabs. But no culinary catastrophe comes close to the miserable pickings offered by your average rail buffet car. Remembering that half of what is mislabelled a menu is missing at any one time, one is usually offered a bleak selection of stale sandwiches, tepid bacon rolls, unimaginative crisps and depressing paninis. Before boarding a long-distance train today, I always make a packed lunch.

I wasn’t always so dour. I was a train-mad tot, raised between Thomas the Tank Engine, toy tracks and weekend Tube trips. As I grew older, I wept over pictures of disused stations, put railway posters up with my Margaret Thatcher and Jenna Coleman portraits, and re-watched John Betjeman’s Metro-Land with a fanaticism that would put many a jihadi to shame. But the fantasy has died as my professional obligations have made train travel a daily dread.

It shouldn’t have to be like this. We did once know how to make the experience enjoyable. Travelling by rail through Britain should be joyous. Even if one has to stop off at a crime against architecture grotesquely mislabelled as a station, one yearns to see our country’s magnificence while hurtling between destinations in far greater comfort than a motorway slog. An obvious ground zero for rail restorationism is the dining car.

Anyone who has seen North by Northwest or From Russia with Love can attest to the inherent charm of an old-fashioned dining car. Who wouldn’t want a long-distance railway journey enhanced by tucking in to a three-course meal with a decent claret or three? Who wouldn’t rather enjoy a cocktail as the country passes by than a lukewarm lager or oddly frosty coffee? Luxury train journeys with dining experiences – like the Northern Belle, branded “Britain’s poshest train”, and available for booking for Christmas dinner – are doing great business. Why not bring this back to the masses?

There is a 150-odd year tradition of doing so. The Pullman dining car is synonymous with luxury. Originally built by the Pullman Company for dining, sleeping and lounge use, the term still applies to a few remaining high-end train dining services. But the number of them has shrunk precipitously. When the railways were privatised in 1995 almost 250 trains a day offered a dining car. Within two decades, that had shrunk to only four. Privatisation saw passenger numbers double and investment increase nine-old, but at the cost of making journeys soulless.

With Labour’s renationalisation push, there is a chance for a reset. Yet the first returns of renewed state-control are predictably unpromising, with delays and cancellations on South Western Railway rising by 50 per cent since nationalisation. Labour’s technocrats lack the vision required to restore our railways. Which is why the cause of the dining car should be taken up by a politician with a far better appreciation of a long lunch’s merits: Nigel Farage.

The Reform leader may prefer private helicopter to rail. But with his newfound penchant for nationalisation, Farage is already fishing in this pool. What could be more appropriate than pledging to put a Boisdale’s on every inter-city service? Back in 2010, Ukip pledged to “make the Circle line a circle again”. That is the spirit in which Farage shall save our trains.

William Atkinson is assistant content editor at The Spectator

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.
