data.title: Renters’ Rights Act: What the new law means for renters and landlords
data.description:
The Renters’ Rights Bill has become the Renters’ Rights Act, after it received Royal Assent yesterday.

The Government must next set out its plans to implement the changes and bring the law into force.

Tenants who rent in the private sector will receive greater protections from eviction, unfair rent increases and greater stability of tenure.

The controversial body of legislation is the biggest change to renting in over 30 years, according to industry body The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA).

Here, Telegraph Money explains the measures laid out in the bill, what that means for landlords and when it is likely to become law.

  • Renters’ Rights Act key points
  • What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?
  • New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act
  • How will it affect landlords?
  • Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Renters’ Rights Act key points

  • End of Section 21 “no fault” evictions: instead, landlords wanting to regain possession of a rental property will need to use approved grounds
  • Rent increases can only happen once a year: any increases must reflect market rates
  • New property standards: under the new “Decent Homes Standard”, properties must be safe, of good value and good quality
  • End of fixed-term tenancies: new and existing tenancies must change to rolling agreements
  • New landlord ombudsman and database: will give tenants access to impartial and binding resolution for complaints against landlords. All landlords must sign up
  • New pet rules: tenants have the right to request pets, but landlords can stipulate pet insurance must be taken out to protect from any resulting damage
  • Rental payment overhaul: landlords cannot accept offers above the advertised asking price for rentals, and no more than one month’s rent can be paid in advance.

What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?

The Renters’ Rights Bill built on the previous Renters’ Reform Bill, introduced by the Conservatives. Now the Renters’ Rights Act, it will introduce new protections and rights for the estimated 11 million private tenants in England by reforming the current system of renting.

An end to fixed-term tenancies, longer notice periods to give renters more time to find a new home and restrictions on rent increases will give tenants more rights and landlords less control over how they manage their property and buy-to-let business.

Labour says it will “transform the experience of private renting” which will largely be achieved by ending Section 21 evictions dubbed “no-fault evictions”.

The NRLA has argued the rules could force landlords out of the sector – reinforced by a survey earlier this year by Pegasus Insight, which revealed 37pc of landlords planned to sell a property in the next 12 months while just 6pc said they intended to buy.

New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act

Evictions and serving notice:

  • Section 21 no-fault evictions will be abolished.
  • Fixed-term tenancies will be replaced with periodic tenancies, also known as rolling contracts that don’t have an end date.
  • Tenants will have a 12-month protected period at the start of their tenancy preventing landlords from forcing evictions to move back in or sell.
  • Tenants can serve two months’ notice to quit the property at any time.
  • Landlords must give four months’ notice if they wish to move back in or sell a rental property.
  • Landlords will not be able to evict a tenant in arrears until three months’ payment have been missed, up from two months while the notice period has been increased from two to four weeks.

Prices and payment:

  • Rent increases are limited to once a year and only to the market rate, which can be challenged by tenants at the first-tier tribunal if they believe it is excessive. Increases cannot be backdated.
  • Large amounts of rent cannot be demanded in advance of a tenancy starting.
  • Landlords must register with a digital database, failing to do so means that they cannot use certain grounds of possession.
  • Asking rents must be published to prevent bidding wars, making it illegal for landlords to accept offers higher than the rent advertised.

Standards and safety:

  • The Decent Homes Standard and Awaab’s Law, currently applicable to homes in the social housing sector, will be extended to private sector landlords to ensure homes are made safe, are of good value and good quality.
  • A private rented sector landlord ombudsman will be set up to provide an impartial and binding resolution for tenant complaints against landlords.

Discrimination:

  • Strengthened rights for tenants to have pets, although landlords can insist on pet insurance to cover any damage to their property.
  • End discrimination against tenants on benefits or with children.

Compliance:

  • Councils will have the power to issue civil penalties of up to £7,000 to landlords who don’t comply with the reforms, rising to £40,000 for a serious breach or repeat non-compliance with the law.
  • More strength will be given to rent repayment orders that can be issued by councils when landlords breach the law.

How will it affect tenants?

The Renters’ Rights Act will give tenants greater protections and stronger rights when they’re renting.

As outlined in the rules above, among the changes the new law will usher in, tenants must be given longer notice periods when their landlords want to move back in or sell the property they’re renting, the property must legally reach the Decent Homes Standard, and they will no longer be subject to frequent rent increases.

For any tenants with a complaint about their landlord, the introduction of an independent ombudsman will help readdress the natural power dynamic and help tenants to reach a resolution.

How will it affect landlords?

Sylvie Harris, of property consultancy INHOUS, said: “Through the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions, landlords will only be able to end tenancies using specific grounds under Section 8, such as rent arrears or anti-social behaviour or if they intend to sell or move into the property.”

For the latter, an extended four-month notice period must be served.

But the pressure this will place on a court system, which Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, acknowledged during a parliamentary debate was “on its knees”, is raising concerns and has led to an amendment being tabled to review the impact of this plan on the legal system.

When the Conservative government brought forward its draft renters rights legislation, then-minister Michael Gove said that the right to make no-fault evictions would remain in place until a full review of the courts had been completed.

Currently, the average time between a landlord submitting a claim for an eviction and regaining possession of their property is more than seven months. In response to the Government’s claims that the courts will be ready for the impact of the changes, Ben Beadle, chief executive of the NRLA, said: “Seven months is an eternity for responsible landlords who may be dealing with serious rent arrears and for neighbours having to endure anti-social behaviour.”

Not only could it take longer to evict a tenant, landlords will have to endure greater levels of arrears before they apply for possession as the minimum level of arrears for eviction is bumped up from two to three months.

Landlords operating in the student let sector could also face challenges in operating their businesses efficiently if the legislation is implemented in its current form.

Ending fixed-term tenancies removes the certainty for these landlords that their property will be available to let to a new batch of students when the academic year begins.

Although grounds for possession have been included, which allows landlords of houses in multiple occupation (HMOs) to evict last year’s students to make way for a new group, one and two-bedroom student flats were not mentioned.

Banning the receipt of large sums of rent upfront, meanwhile, could put landlords off renting to international students or tenants who have had credit difficulties in the past or do not pass affordability assessments. Without the security of payment in advance, these groups may struggle to be accepted.

When will the bill become law?

The Government has announced May 1 2026 as the date when Section 21 evictions will be outlawed in England. Also from this date, fixed-term tenancy contracts must be replaced by ‘rolling’ contracts, there’ll be an end to bidding wars and landlords must include clearer rules on pets.

Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Yes, it will. We are still waiting to find out the finer details, but the plan is to make the changes to tenancies in one go rather than create a two-tier system that applies the rules to new and existing tenancies at different times.

data.title: Rachel Reeves warned of business exodus after Budget
data.description:
Rachel Reeves will suffer an exodus of talent to the US unless she addresses a crisis facing the UK’s fastest-growing businesses in Wednesday’s Budget, Labour backers have warned.

In an open letter to the Chancellor on the eve of the Budget, a Labour MP, pressure groups and business leaders urged Ms Reeves to tackle the “valley of death” facing UK entrepreneurs.

It warned that current growth incentives were “inadequate” and contributing to a “structural failure” in the UK’s investment climate that was damaging the economy.

The letter was spearheaded by Sarah Edwards, a Labour MP who sits on the Commons business and trade committee, and coordinated by the Good Growth Foundation. It warned that the move was forcing successful companies to look overseas for the funding needed to expand.

This was particularly the case in regions outside London and the South East, the letter added.

“This failure of retention – the ‘valley of death’ for high-growth firms – means that companies that have survived the start-up phase are frequently forced to look overseas for the capital required to expand,” the letter warned.

“This market failure inadvertently contributes to regional inequality, failing to translate local research excellence into good jobs and local prosperity.”

The letter is also signed by Joe Jervis, the head of Labour Renaissance, which was founded by Labour MP Stephen Kinnock and is funded privately by party members.

Yvette Cooper, the Foreign Secretary, and Justin Madders, a former employment minister, have also sat on the group’s advisory board.

SME4Labour, whose fundraisers have been attended by senior Labour figures including Sir Keir Starmer, is also a signatory alongside Praful Nargund, director of the Good Growth Foundation as well as several business leaders.

While the letter does not directly name the US, businesses large and small have fled to the world’s biggest economy, which has seen as having much deeper capital markets and more generous funding arrangements.

The group calls for the current Enterprise Investment Scheme (EIS) to be scrapped and replaced with a new regime costing £650m offering more generous tax breaks and relief to a broader number of companies to incentivise entrepreneurs to keep their businesses in Britain.

The letter adds: “It is our collective conviction that the upcoming Budget must prioritise support for the hard grafters behind these businesses – the engines of aspiration, pride and opportunity in every corner of the country.

“By doing so, the Government can deliver on its pledge to be on the side of workers, both the employed and the self-employed. When we grow, the economy grows.”

Business confidence has plummeted over the year following Ms Reeves’s record tax raid in October 2024.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, admitted on Monday that Labour’s tax rises have led to an exodus of wealthy Britons.

Mr Kyle conceded that “some of the decisions” made by the Government since taking office had caused “some people to leave”.

Ms Reeves’s non-dom raid has also triggered an exodus of some of Britain’s richest people.

Lakshmi Mittal, the steel magnate who is worth more than £15bn according to the Sunday Times Rich List, is said to have moved his tax residence from the UK to Switzerland and will spend most of his time in Dubai.

A Treasury spokesman declined to comment.

data.title: British Airways owner threatens to shift investment to Madrid amid Heathrow row
data.description:
The chief executive of British Airways (BA) has warned that its Spanish owner could ditch investment in Heathrow if the bill for the airport’s third runway is too high.

Sean Doyle said International Airlines Group (IAG) is concerned by the cost of Heathrow’s £49bn expansion, which will be largely paid for by carriers and passengers.

He said IAG does not support plans to move the M25 to accommodate the expansion, while he also urged officials to consider pursuing a shorter, cheaper runway.

A failure to keep passenger fees under control risks making Heathrow less attractive, Mr Doyle said, while it could also lead to IAG prioritising investment in its airlines overseas, including Spain’s Iberia and Ireland’s Aer Lingus.

He said: “We’re part of a group and the group allocates capital on the basis of performance.

“If Heathrow becomes uncompetitive and that impacts the performance of an airline like BA, there’ll be opportunities to put that capital elsewhere.”

Mr Doyle added: “Madrid is flying at the minute, with Iberia reporting very strong results, and Barcelona is an expanding market we have a very strong presence in. Dublin has built a new runway for a couple of billion, so there’s headroom there.

“There will be debates about competitiveness and airport charges when it comes to the way IAG allocates capital. We’re not at that point yet but that’s why, for everybody’s sake, a competitive Heathrow is very important.”

British Airways stepped up its campaign against escalating costs at Heathrow as the Government prepares to choose between competing runway plans by the end of the month.

Heathrow Airport’s own proposals call for the construction of a 3.5km runway built over the M25, lifting annual passenger numbers from 82 million to 150 million.

A rival plan from Surinder Arora, the hotel tycoon, envisages a 2,800m strip that would be unable to take the very biggest jets when fully laden.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves have put airport expansion at the heart of Labour’s bid to kickstart the economy, arguing that the pursuit of growth “trumps” net zero commitments.

Labour is seeking to speed up legal processes in a bid to secure planning permission for a third runway before the next general election.

Mr Doyle said BA accepts that Heathrow is full but questioned whether the airport’s own model is unnecessarily complex.

He said: “We need to make sure we’re competitive and build this runway as quickly and efficiently as we can, and any opportunity to achieve that goal should be explored.

“If you can avoid moving the M25, you should avoid moving the M25. You can’t argue against the logic of that. And I think we should look at ways of potentially building a shorter runway.”

‘Serious calculations’

Thomas Woldbye, Heathrow’s chief executive, hit back at the claims, dismissing Mr Doyle’s suggestion that building over the motorway may be unnecessary.

He said: “I agree with him – if we could avoid it, we should – but it is not avoidable. It’s expensive but it’s a well-controlled, known technology solution.

“If you build a shorter runway, you have to move more motorways, like the M4 spur, which is more complicated. It’s in several layers with a railway underneath and you have nowhere to put your motorways or your building site and soil.”

He said Mr Arora’s plan would require demolition of more than 2,000 homes, compared with only about 750 for Heathrow’s own proposals, and that the difference had not been costed.

Mr Woldbye said that could make a shorter runway more expensive and that “serious calculations” indicated that only a full-length runway would keep longer-term costs to a minimum.

He said: “You’d end up with much less capacity. That does not make sense. If you want lower fares, you need the maximum number of passengers to spread costs.”

The chief executive said that Heathrow would nevertheless look at any decision from Labour recommending a cheaper alternative to its blueprint.

Asked if the airport could guarantee that costs for expansion would be capped at £49bn, Mr Woldbye said that “naturally I cannot guarantee that” since the runway would not be ready for a decade and a contingency of 20-30pc “is normal”.

He added: “We have no reason to be more expensive than we should be. We are a regulated entity, our interest is to be as efficient as we possibly can.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Daily Mail owner in exclusive talks to buy The Telegraph for £500m
data.description:
The Daily Mail owner Lord Rothermere has entered exclusive discussions to acquire The Telegraph for £500m.

Lord Rothermere’s holding company DMGT said it “plans to invest substantially in Telegraph Media Group with the aim of accelerating its international expansion”.

The company is in talks with RedBird IMI, the joint venture between the United Arab Emirates and the US private equity firm RedBird Capital Partners.

They made their own tortured two-year bid for control of The Telegraph that collapsed last week amid continuing concerns over potential foreign state influence.

Lord Rothermere had lined up as a minority investor alongside RedBird and the UAE, and had been expected to take a stake of just 10pc. DMGT has longstanding business connections in the UAE via its oil and gas information arm.

DMGT said its takeover would comply with regulations on foreign state influence as “there will be no foreign state investment or capital in the funding structure”.

The planned addition of The Telegraph to DMGT’s existing portfolio, which, as well as The Daily and Sunday Mail titles, includes the i, Metro and New Scientist, is likely to trigger calls for an in-depth investigation of the impact on media plurality. The acquisition could leave Lord Rothermere in control of around half the national newspaper market.

It may prompt calls for him to sell either or both of Metro and the i.

DMGT said it was “confident any regulatory process can be concluded swiftly and positively”.

“Today’s media landscape is unrecognisable from a decade ago. News publishers have to compete against both vast global online platforms and myriad digital and social media news sources, some of them highly unreliable,” the group said in a statement.

‘I have long admired The Daily Telegraph’

Sky is expected to make similar arguments about the rise of tech giants in its ongoing attempted takeover of ITV’s broadcasting business, although concerns there mostly surround competition in advertising sales rather than the public interest in plurality of ownership.

The swift announcement of an agreement between RedBird, the UAE and DMGT comes days after peers demanded that Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, impose a transparent and independent sale process to guard against further uncertainty.

They echoed concerns raised in a Telegraph leader column that a foreign state should not be allowed to decide its next owner. It had emerged that a “poison pill” debt threatened to leave The Telegraph in unsustainable debt to the UAE.

It is almost two years since RedBird IMI attempted to seize control of The Telegraph by paying off the Barclay family’s overdue debts to Lloyds Banking Group. 

The surprise move derailed an auction but was blocked in April last year following a parliamentary outcry over press freedom.

The partners then made a second attempt with a restructured £500m bid in which US private equity firm RedBird Capital, moved to take control of the publication. It would have made RedBird the majority owner and granted the UAE a 15pc stake. 

However the bid collapsed last week amid ongoing concerns around the UAE’s involvement and RedBird’s Chinese links. Ms Nandy had been considering whether to refer the consortium to Ofcom and the Competition and Markets Authority. 

Lord Rothermere said: “I have long admired The Daily Telegraph. My family and I have an enduring love of newspapers and for the journalists who make them. The Daily Telegraph is Britain’s largest and best quality broadsheet newspaper, and I have grown up respecting it.”

He expressed support for The Telegraph’s editorial leadership, which had objected to RedBird IMI.

“Chris Evans is an excellent editor, and we intend to give him the resources to invest in the newsroom. Under our ownership, The Daily Telegraph will become a global brand, just as The Daily Mail has,” he said.

A spokesman for RedBird IMI said: “DMGT and RedBird IMI have worked swiftly to reach the agreement announced today, which will shortly be submitted to the Secretary of State.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ozempic maker wins High Court ban on fake fat jab websites
data.description:
The drugmaker behind Wegovy has secured a major victory after the High Court granted it a blocking order for websites selling fake versions of its weight-loss jabs.

Novo Nordisk will be able to force internet providers to block access to four websites that the company accused of selling “fake and unauthorised forms of semaglutide”, the ingredient which helps people lose weight.

It claimed the websites were selling counterfeit versions of Ozempic, the drugmaker’s diabetes injection, which is used off-label for weight loss.

Novo Nordisk warned the counterfeit drugs “pose a risk to health” and were not regulated, adding: “The substances [the websites] offer to supply have not been evaluated by the UK MHRA [Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency] as being safe or of suitable quality.”

GPs have warned that people are “dicing with death” by using fake injections, with reports of drugs being wrapped up in kitchen roll without instructions when they are sent to customers.

Earlier this month, an investigation found copycat versions were being sold on Facebook and TikTok, with patients who took them reportedly losing vision and vomiting repeatedly. Both social media companies said the sale of weight-loss injections was prohibited under their policies, with TikTok removing searches and hashtags for the drugs after the investigation.

However, there are growing fears that more people could start hunting out dangerous fakes after a major jump in the price of one of the biggest approved obesity jabs.

In September, US drugmaker Eli Lilly increased the UK price of Mounjaro – known as the King Kong of weight-loss injections – by as much as 170pc following pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, to lower the cost for Americans.

It means that the drug now costs as much as £330 per injection pen, based on the highest dose. Each injection pen includes four doses. Previously, the highest-dose injection pen cost £122.

Drug crime specialists at the time warned that the increase was likely to lead to a jump in the number of copycat drugs being imported from Chinese labs and sold illicitly in the UK.

Novo Nordisk has not increased its prices. At an online pharmacy, patients can expect to pay between £99 and £199 for four doses.

It shipped in more supply of its weight-loss jabs to Britain following the price increase at its rival, after seeing more demand.

A spokesman for Novo Nordisk said of the blocking order: “We are pleased that the English High Court has confirmed the availability of website blocking as a remedy to tackle the problem of counterfeit and unauthorised semaglutide.

“This remedy is an innovative and powerful tool for taking effective action and forms an important part of the suite of enforcement remedies available against makers and sellers of unlawful products.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Daily Mail owner given three weeks to finalise Telegraph bid
data.description:
The owner of the Daily Mail has been given three weeks to finalise its £500m bid for The Telegraph.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, set the deadline for Lord Rothermere’s holding company DMGT to present its formal plans for consideration, branding the limbo facing this newspaper “unsustainable”.

It has been 19 months since RedBird IMI, a venture mostly backed by the United Arab Emirates, told ministers it would sell The Telegraph after its own bid was blocked over press freedom concerns. The saga began two-and-a-half years ago when the Barclay family lost control to Lloyds Banking Group as a result of their overdue debts.

DMGT announced on Saturday that it had begun exclusive talks towards a £500m deal with RedBird IMI, without giving details of how it intended to structure or fund a transaction.

Ms Nandy’s intervention means the pair must now present their plans to her by mid-December. She said she intended to “build a constructive path toward a timely sale, without further delay, that is in the public interest”.

The Culture Secretary added: “My particular concerns are that the protracted uncertainty has been detrimental to the stability of The Telegraph and its staff, and to the investment appeal of the sector as a whole.”

Lord Rothermere’s company, which owns Metro and The i, as well as the Mail newspaper titles, said it plans to “invest substantially in Telegraph Media Group, with the aim of accelerating its international expansion”.

He has stepped in after previously seeking a 10pc stake in The Telegraph as part of a consortium led by RedBird Capital Partners, the US private equity firm that is the junior partner in RedBird IMI. 

It pulled out this month as it faced continuing concern about its funding, potential foreign state influence and its plans for The Telegraph.

It was the latest in a series of failures by RedBird IMI to exit its investment, which was itself made at pace in late 2023 to derail a competitive auction overseen by Lloyds. 

The UAE-backed venture paid off the Barclay family’s debts and took security over The Telegraph, hoping to convert its position into control.

DMGT has said it will comply with laws on foreign state ownership as there will be “no foreign state investment or capital in the funding structure”.

Ms Nandy warned that any future owner of The Telegraph must be “completely free” of prohibited foreign state influence.

She said she would also exercise her powers to uphold the public interest, which in newspaper mergers surrounds accurate news, free expression of opinion and plurality.

Lord Rothermere and RedBird IMI will require Ms Nandy’s permission for any transaction to proceed, and are likely to face investigation by Ofcom and potentially the Competition and Markets Authority of its impact on media plurality in the UK.

The combination of The Telegraph and DMGT would account for about half the newspaper market.

Lord Rothermere is expected to argue that the plurality of online news sources and tech distribution platforms would guard against an unhealthy concentration of influence. It has been speculated that he could sell The i and Metro to help assuage some of the concerns.

The 57-year-old, whose family fortune was founded in the 19th century newspaper boom, also said he would maintain The Telegraph’s editorial independence from the Daily Mail and invest in international growth.

Plurality debates are likely to focus on the effect that ownership of The Telegraph and the Daily Mail under one umbrella might have. 

DMGT’s 2019 takeover of The i was waved through by Ofcom partly because the newspaper was found to offer a different viewpoint to the Daily Mail.

Ms Nandy said that The Telegraph’s editorial independence must continue to be protected until its ownership is resolved.

data.title: Ozempic maker suffers boardroom exodus after row with shareholder
data.description:
The wealthy foundation behind Ozempic maker Novo Nordisk has seized control of the company in a boardroom coup after a row over the struggling group’s turnaround.

On Monday, the pharmaceutical giant said chairman Helge Lund and six independent directors would resign and be replaced by a group hand-picked by its biggest shareholder, the Novo Nordisk Foundation.

The drugmaker, which makes the anti-obesity jab Wegovy and Ozempic, said the decision came after a disagreement between the board and the foundation over a plan to rejuvenate the Danish company’s flagging fortunes.

The Novo Nordisk Foundation is the wealthiest charity in the world with £123bn in assets and the company’s largest shareholder, controlling 77pc of Novo.

The foundation has nominated five new directors to be appointed at an emergency meeting next month and a sixth to be appointed next year.

Lars Rebien Sørensen, the former chief executive of Novo, has been nominated to replace Mr Lund as chairman. Mr Lund recently left his position chairing BP after a disastrous attempt to focus the company on green energy.

Novo’s exodus amounts to the entire board, besides the foundation’s own investment boss and the four members elected by employees.

The rift between the company’s top shareholders and its board came to the fore after a slump in Novo’s performance.

The company makes the diabetes drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, which are used for weight loss. Both contain the ingredient semaglutide, which stimulates weight loss by making people feel full.

The initial success of the drugs led Novo to become Europe’s most valuable company, but shares have fallen by two thirds as it has faced competition from Eli Lilly, which makes rival drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound.

Novo’s own successor to Wegovy disappointed in weight loss trials, while the Trump administration has put pressure on the company to cut prices.

“Following dialogue with the Novo Nordisk Foundation regarding the future composition of the board of directors, it has not been possible to reach a common understanding,” Novo said.

“The board proposed a renewal focusing on [the] addition of select, new competencies while also maintaining continuity, whereas the board of the foundation wanted a more extensive reconfiguration.”

It said the new board’s first priority would be “implementing its transformation plans and regaining the company’s growth momentum”.

Mr Sørensen said: “Given the fast-moving environment in which Novo Nordisk operates, we believe that it is in the best interest of the company and its shareholders to implement a board renewal as soon as possible, rather than wait until the ordinary annual general meeting in March next year.”

He said the foundation fully supported the company’s new chief executive, Mike Doustdar, who replaced ousted boss Lars Fruergaard Jørgensen earlier this year, as well as recent plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

Novo’s shares fell by 2.4pc after the announcement.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: China’s property crisis reignited as state-backed builder fights to avoid default
data.description:
China’s property crisis has reignited after one of its largest state-backed developers was plunged into a fresh debt spiral.

Vanke, once the country’s largest housebuilder, slumped in the bond market on Wednesday amid fears it cannot pay back lenders without government support.

Some of its Chinese-listed bonds plunged by more than 20pc, forcing the Shenzhen Stock Exchange to suspend trading, while its shares also fell 6pc to 3.88 Hong Kong dollars (37p) – the lowest level this year.

Vanke is facing a race against time to convince investors it can repay 13.4bn yuan (£1.4bn) of debts due for repayment next June.

Viewed as a bellwether for China’s property sector, the company’s woes underscore the pressure on the wider market as Beijing attempts to prop up its housing sector.

Demand has remained weak, with new home sales dropping at their fastest monthly pace during October.

This is despite efforts from the Chinese government to stimulate growth, introducing policies to ease curbs on buying in cities and bring down borrowing costs on existing mortgages.

It comes after another property giant, Evergrande Group, delisted from Hong Kong’s stock exchange in August.

The downfall of Evergrande marked the biggest collapse of the sector-wide slump, which had dragged down the country’s economic growth.

Analysts at major banks predict there is more gloom ahead for China’s property market. UBS said it expected property sales, construction activity and investment to drop further for at least two more years.

In October, Fitch Ratings estimated that new home sales could potentially fall by another 15pc to 20pc from their current level before the market stabilises in 2026.

Vanke this year disclosed the sudden departures of Yu Liang, its chairman, and Zhu Jiusheng, its chief executive, citing “work adjustment reasons” for Mr Yu and “health reasons” for Mr Zhu.

At the same time, Vanke said it was experiencing “temporary liquidity difficulties” from a fall in new-build home sales in China. It said it expected to make a loss of 45bn yuan for 2024, dropping from 12bn yuan profit in 2023.

Policymakers in Beijing have reportedly been considering fresh measures to turn around the market’s fortunes, including subsidising interest payments on new mortgages and lowering transaction fees on home sales.

Vanke was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Sainsbury’s to ‘nudge’ delivery drivers to wear seatbelts
data.description:
Sainsbury’s will start “nudging” its delivery drivers to wear seatbelts as it installs new cameras in vans to track workers’ movements.

The supermarket has told its drivers that it was placing new selfie cameras inside their vans to help “prioritise safer driving”.

Starting in the new year, Sainsbury’s said these cameras would start giving drivers real-time safety alerts or audio “nudges”. This would include reprimanding them for forgotten seatbelts or flagging any distractions.

This new system would “give [drivers] a chance to take corrective action before an incident occurs”, Sainsbury’s said.

Initially, the cameras will not issue audio warnings, but will turn on whenever vehicles are moving and for 10 minutes after they have come to a stop. Footage will be saved if a traffic accident occurs. From next year, the cameras will also start recording and logging footage if they detect any risk, such as drivers not wearing seatbelts.

Sainsbury’s insisted the change was “not about monitoring people”, but about keeping drivers safe. It added that the technology was currently still in a trial phase.

However, union chiefs hit out at the use of so-called “spy cameras”.

Nadine Houghton, GMB’s national officer, said driver-facing cameras represented “an intrusion of privacy and unnecessary surveillance of delivery drivers”, adding: “We can understand why employers may turn to this technology but we would caution against quick-fix solutions.”

She said safety could instead be improved on the road if drivers had “adequate time to complete their deliveries” and were “fully trained”.

Delivery drivers told The Telegraph on Wednesday that they were frustrated over the decision to spend money on the technology, which they claimed could be used for investing in preventing attacks on staff.

Tesco last month rolled out body cameras to more than 5,000 delivery drivers so that they could record abusive customers.

More than three quarters of delivery drivers for chains including Asda, Tesco, Sainsbury’s and Morrisons have been abused over the past year, Usdaw figures have shown.

As well as body-cams, Tesco has also given delivery drivers “spit kits” that gather DNA and can be used as evidence.

In the memo to workers, seen by The Telegraph, Sainsbury’s said the cameras would help it “stand firmly behind any driver who is wrongly accused of causing an incident, giving us the evidence we need to protect fairness and support our colleagues”.

Bally Auluk, the Usdaw national officer, said the union was “monitoring [the trial of technology in Sainsbury delivery vans] closely to make sure it delivers the promised safety benefits and security for staff”.

He added: “We cannot underestimate the dangers delivery drivers face on the road and we welcome measures that employers introduce to help mitigate the risks.

“Importantly, we want it to be clear to everyone that cameras are monitoring vans and we urge the public to show the respect our members deserve, they are key workers literally delivering an essential service.”

The decision by Sainsbury’s to install driver cameras comes after surveys have suggested that delivery workers are less likely to wear their seatbelts than the general public.

Official statistics suggest that only 0.9pc of car drivers do not wear a seatbelt while in the car, while drivers of other vehicles, including vans, is 6.3pc.

Under UK law, delivery drivers are required to wear a seatbelt unless they are travelling no more than 50 metres between stops.

Other supermarkets have previously looked at installing driver-facing cameras in their vans. Earlier this year, Asda said it was considering using AI cameras to detect small movements from drivers and issue audio warnings if any risk was identified.

However, in June, the supermarket dropped the plans following opposition from unions, who had raised concerns that it breached workers’ privacy.

Asda said it paused the proposal so it could focus on other projects, as it embarks on a push to turn around the ailing grocer.

A spokesman for Sainsbury’s said: “As part of our continued commitment to colleague safety while they are out on the road, we’re trialling new technology in a small number of our grocery delivery vehicles.

“It provides real-time hazard alerts, which helps prevent incidents. This kind of safety system is already widely used by delivery companies to help prevent road traffic accidents and its carefully controlled cameras only switch on if a potential incident is detected.”
data.author: The telegraph
